lenten rose: A lenten rose has a large, cup-shaped or saucer-shaped, nodding flower with five broad, petal-like sepals.
A lenten rose features a central cluster of numerous, prominent, pale green or yellowish stamens that contrast with the main sepal color.
It has thick, leathery, evergreen leaves that are palmate or pedate, meaning they are shaped like a hand with several toothed leaflets.
A lenten rose is known for its subtle, often dusty or muted color palette, including shades of mauve, dusky pink, deep purple, creamy white, and greenish-white.
The sepals, which look like petals, are thick and long-lasting, often featuring delicate speckles, freckles, or veining in a darker color.
Visually, it is a low-growing, clump-forming plant with downward-facing flowers that hang gracefully from thick, fleshy stems.
The flowers have a waxy, substantial texture and a simple, open-faced form, typically with five large, rounded sepals.
A lenten rose looks like a wild, single-petaled rose, but with a more cup-like shape and a prominent, brushy center of stamens.
The colors are often complex and antique-looking, with many blooms showing a picotee edge or a darker color on the reverse of the sepals.
It is a very early blooming perennial, with its flowers often appearing above its dark, leathery, umbrella-like leaves in late winter or early spring.
You can identify a lenten rose by its downward-nodding, cup-shaped flowers that appear in late winter or early spring.
Identify it by its thick, leathery, evergreen, hand-shaped (palmate) leaves that are often serrated.

You can identify a lenten rose by its unique, often speckled or freckled coloration in muted, dusky shades of purple, pink, green, and white.

barbeton daisy: A barbeton daisy is a cheerful flower with a classic daisy structure: a single or double row of long, slender petals radiating from a prominent central disc.
It has a large, colorful flower head, typically 2 to 5 inches across, with numerous, fine, strap-shaped ray florets (petals).
A barbeton daisy, or Gerbera, is famous for its intensely saturated, vibrant colors, including brilliant red, orange, yellow, hot pink, and pure white.
The central disc is often a contrasting color, such as black, dark brown, green, or yellow, creating a striking 'eye'.
The flower sits atop a long, thick, leafless, fuzzy green stem, which holds the large bloom high.

daffodil: A daffodil is a flower with a unique and distinctive structure, consisting of a central cup or trumpet-shaped corona surrounded by a ring of six petal-like tepals (the perianth).
It has a star-shaped outer layer of six flat, pointed petals, and a prominent, protruding, central bell or trumpet.
A daffodil is most commonly seen in shades of bright, sunny yellow, but can also be pure white, cream, or bicolored with a yellow trumpet and white outer petals.
The plant grows from a bulb and has long, flat, strap-like or grass-like leaves that are typically a bluish-green color.
The flower sits atop a smooth, leafless, hollow stem (a scape), and the bud is initially enclosed in a papery sheath or spathe.
Visually, a daffodil is the iconic flower of spring, with its cheerful colors and unique, two-part structure.
The central cup or trumpet can vary greatly in size and shape, from a long, narrow tube to a short, wide, frilly-edged cup.
The flower often nods or hangs at a slight angle from the top of the stem.
A daffodil looks like a small, star-shaped plate with a beautifully crafted teacup placed in its center.
The six outer petals are arranged in two whorls of three, creating a symmetrical, six-pointed star behind the central corona.
You can identify a daffodil by its unmistakable structure: a central trumpet or cup (corona) surrounded by a ring of six outer petals (perianth).
Identify it by its long, flat, blade-like, blue-green leaves that grow from the base of the plant.
Look for the single, leafless, smooth flower stalk that supports the bloom.
You can identify a daffodil by its classic spring colors of yellow and white.
The papery, brown sheath (spathe) that the bud emerges from, which then remains at the base of the flower, is a good identifier.
Its growth from a bulb, often in large, naturalized drifts, is a characteristic feature.
The nodding posture of the flower head is typical for most daffodil varieties.
Unlike a tulip, which is a simple cup of six petals, the daffodil's two-part structure of cup-and-saucer is a key difference.
The hollow, cylindrical stem is another feature that helps in its identification.
You can identify different types by the shape of the corona: trumpet daffodils have a cup that is longer than the petals, while cupped daffodils have a shorter cup.

sword lily: A sword lily is a tall, dramatic plant with a one-sided spike of large, funnel-shaped flowers.
It has long, flat, sword-shaped or blade-like leaves that are arranged in a flat fan at the base of the plant.
The flowers of a sword lily, or gladiolus, are large and often have ruffled or frilly edges, blooming in a dense, upright spike.
The florets are arranged alternately up one side of the stiff, thick stem, all facing in roughly the same direction.
A sword lily comes in a vast spectrum of colors, including vibrant reds, pinks, oranges, yellows, purples, and whites, often with intricate blotches or markings in the throat.
Visually, it is a plant of impressive height and bold, architectural form, a towering spire of color.
Each individual flower is trumpet-shaped, with six petals (tepals) that are often of unequal size, creating a slightly asymmetrical, orchid-like appearance.
The plant grows from a corm, and its most defining visual feature is the strong, vertical line created by its flower spike.
A sword lily looks like a magnificent, floral spear or sword, with its sharp leaves and its tall, weapon-like flower stalk.
The flowers bloom sequentially from the bottom of the spike to the top, so the tip of the spike is often a tapered point of green, unopened buds.
You can identify a sword lily by its tall, erect spike of large, trumpet-shaped flowers that are arranged up one side of the stem.
Identify it by its distinctive, flat, sword-shaped leaves that are arranged in a fan-like plane.
Look for the sequential blooming pattern, with open flowers at the bottom and pointed buds at the top of the spike.
The large, often ruffled, and brightly colored florets are a key identifier.
You can identify a sword lily by its impressive height; it is one of the tallest and most dramatic cut flowers.
The combination of the sword-like leaves and the tall, one-sided flower raceme is definitive.
Unlike an iris, which has a more complex flower structure, the gladiolus has simpler, open-faced, funnel-shaped blooms.
The thick, sturdy, unbranched central stalk that supports the many heavy blooms is a good clue.
Look for the colorful markings, splotches, or 'throats' of a contrasting color deep inside the funnel of the flower.
Its growth from a flattened, tunic-covered corm is another, less visible, identifying characteristic.

poinsettia: A poinsettia is a plant famous for its large, colorful, petal-like leaves, which are actually modified leaves called bracts.
The most iconic feature is the star-shaped whorl of brilliant red bracts at the top of the plant.
The true flowers of a poinsettia are the tiny, inconspicuous, bead-like, yellow or green structures located in the very center of the colorful bracts.
A poinsettia has large, simple, ovate, dark green leaves below the colorful bracts.
The colorful bracts are not always red; they can also be white, pink, marbled, or speckled.
Visually, a poinsettia is the quintessential Christmas plant, its star-like form and festive colors making it an iconic holiday decoration.
The bracts are arranged in a rosette or star shape, with the largest ones on the outside and smaller ones closer to the center.
The central cluster of tiny flowers is called a cyathium, and it often has a small, yellow, cup-like gland that produces nectar.
A poinsettia looks like a large, stylized, red flower, but its 'petals' are actually leaves that have changed color.
The plant has a shrubby form, with woody stems that contain a milky white sap.
You can identify a poinsettia by its large, showy, colorful bracts (usually red) that are mistaken for flower petals.
Identify it by looking for the tiny, yellow, bead-like true flowers in the very center of the rosette of colored bracts.
The large, simple, dark green leaves that grow below the colored bracts are a key feature.
You can identify a poinsettia by its association with the Christmas holiday season, as it is ubiquitously sold at that time.
The star-like arrangement of the large, colored bracts is its most recognizable characteristic.
If a stem is broken, the milky white sap that exudes is a good identifier for plants in the Euphorbia family, including poinsettias.
Unlike a true flower, the colored bracts are simply leaves that have changed color in response to specific light cycles.
Look for the combination of the upper colored leaves and the lower green leaves on the same plant.
The central flower cluster (cyathia) is very small and lacks any petals, which distinguishes it from a true flower.
Its typical form as a potted houseplant is how it is most often identified.

bolero deep blue: A bolero deep blue is a flower with multiple layers of soft, swirling petals, resembling a rose or a soft tulip.
It has a cup-shaped or bell-shaped bloom composed of delicate, tissue-paper-like petals in an incredibly deep, rich, violet-blue color.
A bolero deep blue, a variety of lisianthus, has a very elegant, swirling bud that untwists as it opens.
The petals have a soft, satiny sheen and are often slightly ruffled or wavy at the edges.
The plant has smooth, straight, slender stems with waxy, blue-green, oval-shaped leaves arranged in opposite pairs.
Visually, it is a flower of sophisticated and intense color, its deep, velvety blue-violet hue being its most defining feature.
The flower has a very full, layered, and romantic appearance, with the petals forming a soft, spiraling cup.
A bolero deep blue looks like a rose that has been dyed a magnificent, deep, purple-blue color.
The center of the flower often contains a cluster of contrasting yellow stamens, which stand out against the deep blue petals.
Its overall form is one of refined elegance, with the graceful, cup-shaped bloom held aloft on a long, slender stem.
You can identify a bolero deep blue by its deep, saturated, violet-blue color, which is uncommon in rose-like flowers.

The spiraling, pointed bud that untwists as it opens is a key identifier for lisianthus varieties like this one.

wallflower: A wallflower is a plant that produces dense, upright clusters (racemes) of small, four-petaled flowers.
It has a classic cross shape to its flowers, with four rounded or oval petals arranged like an 'X', which is typical of the mustard family.
A wallflower is known for its rich, often warm and earthy colors, including vibrant orange, bronze, deep yellow, brownish-red, and purple.
The flowers are arranged in a terminal spike, which elongates as the lower flowers open and fade, while new buds form at the top.
The plant is often a bushy, somewhat woody perennial or biennial, with simple, narrow, lance-shaped, dark green leaves.
Visually, it is a plant of abundant, rich, and often multi-toned color, with its dense spires of flowers creating a bold statement.
Some varieties are wonderfully bicolored or change color as they age, with a single flower spike displaying shades of orange, purple, and yellow all at once.
A wallflower looks like a vibrant, colorful spire, its small, cross-shaped flowers packed tightly together.
The plant has a slightly wild, informal, cottage-garden appearance, often seen growing in old walls (hence the name).
After flowering, the plant produces long, thin, upright seed pods called siliques, which are also a distinctive feature.
You can identify a wallflower by its dense, spire-like clusters of small, four-petaled flowers.
Identify it by the cross-shaped arrangement of the four petals on each individual flower.
Look for its rich, warm color palette of oranges, yellows, bronzes, and purples.
You can identify a wallflower by its bushy, often woody-based growth habit and its narrow, dark green leaves.
The elongated flower spike, with open flowers below and a tight cluster of buds at the tip, is a key identifier.
Its strong, sweet, and often spicy fragrance is a well-known characteristic.
The combination of four-petaled flowers in a dense raceme on a bushy plant is the best way to identify it.
Unlike a stock flower, which is also a four-petaled spire, a wallflower is typically more branching and shrubby.
Look for the long, thin, bean-like seed pods that stand erect on the stem after the flowers have finished.

marigold: A marigold is a cheerful, pom-pom-like flower with a densely packed head of ruffled, wavy, or pleated petals.
It has a full, globular or flattened-dome flower head, composed of numerous, overlapping ray and disc florets.
A marigold is famous for its warm, vibrant color palette of golden yellow, bright orange, deep red, and rich bronze, often in bicolored patterns.
The plant's most notable non-visual feature, which informs its visual identity, is its pungent, aromatic scent, but visually, its foliage is finely dissected, fern-like, and dark green.
The flower heads can be large and globe-like (African marigolds) or smaller and more button-like (French marigolds).
Visually, it is a sturdy, dependable, and intensely colorful flower, with a texture that is both frilly and dense.
French marigold varieties are often bicolored, with a contrasting color, like red, edging the yellow petals, creating a beautiful, patterned effect.
A marigold looks like a small, frilly, golden sun or a perfectly round, textured button.
The petals are broad and wavy, packed together so tightly that the center of the flower is often completely obscured.
The plant has a bushy, branching habit, and it is typically covered in a profusion of its bright, long-lasting blooms.
You can identify a marigold (Tagetes) by its densely packed, pom-pom-like flower heads in shades of yellow, orange, and red.
Identify it by its finely dissected, fern-like foliage, which has a strong, pungent, and very distinctive aroma when crushed.
Look for the bicolored patterns, especially on French marigolds, where petals are often edged or crested with a contrasting color.
You can identify an African marigold by its very large, fully double, globe-shaped flower heads on tall stems.
You can identify a French marigold by its smaller size, bushier habit, and often flattened, anemone-like or crested flower heads.
The combination of a frilly, pom-pom flower and aromatic, lacy foliage is the key identifier.
Unlike a calendula (English marigold), the Tagetes marigold has much more complex, ruffled flowers and feathery, not simple, leaves.
The sturdy, ribbed, and often reddish-tinged stems are another identifying feature.
Its habit of prolific blooming from summer until frost is a well-known characteristic.
The way the petals are densely layered and ruffled, creating a very full, almost solid-looking flower head, is a primary visual trait.

buttercup: A buttercup is a small, simple flower with five (or sometimes more) glossy, bright yellow petals that form a shallow, cup-like shape.
Its most defining visual characteristic is the intense, waxy sheen on the surface of its yellow petals, which makes them highly reflective, like polished butter.
A buttercup has a simple, radial symmetry, with its five rounded petals arranged neatly around a central cluster of bright yellow stamens and pistils.
The plant is typically low-growing, with dark green leaves that are often deeply lobed or divided into three parts.
The flower is held on a slender, delicate, often hairy green stem that rises from the basal leaves.
Visually, it is the quintessential wildflower of meadows, a small, bright, cheerful speck of liquid gold.
The petals are distinctly cup-shaped, they are not flat, which helps hold the central reproductive parts.
A buttercup looks like a tiny, perfectly formed bowl of solidified sunshine.
Beneath the yellow petals are five smaller, greenish sepals, which are often visible between the petals.
The entire plant has a fresh, green, and slightly wild appearance, often forming dense, golden carpets in fields and lawns.
You can identify a buttercup by its five bright yellow, intensely glossy, cup-shaped petals.
Identify it by the waxy, almost plastic-like sheen on the petals, which is a key characteristic of the Ranunculus genus.
Look for the low-growing habit and the dark green leaves that are often divided into three distinct lobes.
The combination of the simple, five-petaled, cup-like flower and its brilliant, buttery-yellow color is the easiest way to identify it.
You can identify it by the central button of numerous yellow stamens.
Unlike a dandelion, a buttercup has a few distinct, broad petals, not a head made of hundreds of tiny ray florets.
Its typical habitat of damp meadows, pastures, and lawns is a good clue.
The small, simple, almost childlike perfection of the flower is a recognizable feature.
Identify it by its slender, often grooved and slightly hairy stems.
The way the petals reflect light, allowing you to famously 'see if you like butter' by holding it under your chin, is based on its unique glossy cell structure.

oxeye daisy: An oxeye daisy is a classic wildflower with a single, large flower head at the top of a long stem, featuring a ring of pure white petals around a bright yellow central disc.
It has a simple, cheerful appearance, with numerous long, slender, white ray florets radiating from a broad, golden-yellow, button-like center.
An oxeye daisy looks like a larger, wilder version of the common lawn daisy, with one substantial flower head per stem instead of many small ones.
The plant has dark green, toothed, spoon-shaped basal leaves, with smaller, lance-shaped leaves clasping the tall, grooved stem.
Its petals are slightly spoon-shaped and often have a few small notches at the very tip, giving them a subtly fringed look.
Visually, an oxeye daisy is a tall, elegant, yet simple flower, defined by its clean, graphic contrast of white and yellow.
The central disc is a composite of hundreds of tiny, packed, golden-yellow florets, creating a dense, slightly flattened dome.
The flower head is substantial, typically 1 to 2 inches across, and faces directly outwards or slightly upwards on its slender stalk.
An oxeye daisy is a quintessential meadow flower, with its solitary white-and-yellow head dancing on a long stem above the grasses.
It's characterized by its upright, relatively unbranched stem that elevates a single, perfect, classic daisy flower high for pollinators to see.
You can identify an oxeye daisy by its large size and the fact that it typically bears only one flower head per unbranched stem.

Unlike a chamomile, which has feathery leaves, the oxeye daisy has solid, toothed, dark green leaves.

common dandelion: A common dandelion is a familiar wildflower with a bright, sunny yellow flower head composed entirely of numerous, fine, strap-shaped petals (ray florets).
It is famous for its secondary stage: a perfectly spherical, fluffy, white seed head, often called a 'dandelion clock', which consists of many seeds attached to parachute-like pappi.
A dandelion flower grows singly at the top of a smooth, leafless, hollow, cylindrical stem that contains a milky white sap.
The plant's leaves form a basal rosette, lying flat against the ground; they are long, green, and have a distinctive, deeply toothed edge that gives them the name 'lion's tooth'.
The flower head is a composite bloom that looks like a small, shaggy, yellow pom-pom, and it closes up at night or on overcast days.
Visually, it is a plant of two distinct and iconic forms: the golden-yellow sun of its flower, and the ethereal, white globe of its seed head.
The yellow flower head has no central disc; it is made up of only ray florets, giving it a full, uniform appearance.
A dandelion looks like a small, cheerful, yellow powder puff when in bloom, and a delicate, white fairy's wand when it has gone to seed.
The stem is typically smooth, green or tinged with reddish-purple, and exudes a sticky, white, milky latex when broken.
Its entire life is lived low to the ground, with the flower stalks rising from the center of the flat-lying rosette of leaves.
You can identify a common dandelion by its solitary, yellow, multi-petaled flower head on a single, hollow, leafless stalk.
Identify it by its iconic, fluffy, white, spherical seed head or 'clock'.
Look for the basal rosette of long, green leaves with sharp, backward-pointing lobes, resembling a lion's teeth.
Breaking the stem and seeing the milky white sap that emerges is a key identifier.
The combination of a composite yellow flower on a naked stem and a rosette of jagged leaves is definitive.
Unlike a buttercup, a dandelion flower has a shaggy appearance from its many dozens of thin petals, not five broad ones.
Unlike a colt's foot, a dandelion has its leaves present at the time of flowering, and its stem is smooth, not scaly.
Its presence as a common weed in lawns, gardens, and disturbed soil is a clue to its identity.
You can identify it by its powerful, deep taproot, which makes it difficult to pull from the ground.
The way the yellow flower head transforms into the white seed head on the same stalk is a primary identifying life cycle feature.

petunia: A petunia is a flower with a soft, delicate, trumpet-shaped or funnel-shaped blossom and a wide, open mouth.
It features a single or double layer of thin, often slightly sticky petals, which can be smooth or have a beautifully ruffled edge.
A petunia is known for its incredible diversity of colors, including vibrant pinks, purples, reds, whites, and yellows, as well as near-black.
Many varieties have stunning patterns, such as a star shape in a contrasting color, a picotee edge, or intricate, dark veining.
The plant has a mounding or trailing habit, with simple, oval, slightly fuzzy and sticky green leaves.
Visually, a petunia is a plant of abundant, often dramatic color, with its trumpet-like flowers covering the foliage in a dense cascade.
The throat of the flower's trumpet is often a different color or has lines that guide pollinators inside.
A petunia looks like a beautiful, colorful gramophone horn, its wide bell projecting outwards.
The 'double' varieties are packed with a frothy mass of ruffled petals, resembling a carnation or a small peony.
Its overall appearance is one of soft, lush, and continuous bloom, making it a favorite for hanging baskets and containers.
You can identify a petunia by its characteristic funnel or trumpet-shaped flowers with a wide, open face.
Identify it by the slightly sticky or clammy feel of its leaves and stems.
Look for the vast array of colors and intricate patterns, such as stars, veins, and edges, which are hallmarks of modern hybrids.
You can identify a petunia by its growth habit; it is almost always grown as an annual, often spilling out of hanging baskets, window boxes, and pots.
The combination of a simple trumpet flower shape and slightly fuzzy, oval leaves is a good identifier.
Unlike a morning glory, which is a climbing vine, a petunia is typically a mounding or trailing plant that does not climb.
The five-lobed, often fused corolla (the trumpet) is a key structural feature.
Identify 'double' petunias by their extremely full, ruffled, rose-like blooms.

wild pansy: A wild pansy is a small, delicate wildflower with a flat, forward-facing bloom composed of five distinct petals.
It is famous for its tri-color pattern, typically a combination of deep purple on the two upper petals, pale yellow or cream on the two side petals, and a larger, whiskered, yellow lower petal.
A wild pansy has a charming, face-like appearance, with dark, pencil-thin lines, or 'whiskers', radiating from the center over the lower petals.
The five petals are unequal in size and shape: two upper petals often overlap, two side petals point outwards, and one large lower petal provides a landing pad for insects.
The plant is low-growing and can be straggly, with small, oval or heart-shaped, toothed leaves.
Visually, a wild pansy, or heartsease, is a flower of intricate, miniature detail and subtle, charming beauty.
The center of the flower has a bright, nectar-rich, orange-yellow 'eye' where the whisker lines converge.
A wild pansy looks like a tiny, painted face, with two dark 'eyes' (the upper petals), whiskered 'cheeks' (the side petals), and a 'chin' (the lower petal).
The shape is distinctly asymmetrical or bilaterally symmetrical, not radially symmetrical like a daisy.
Its overall appearance is that of a delicate, unassuming, yet beautifully patterned small field flower.
You can identify a wild pansy by its small, five-petaled flower with a distinct, three-color pattern (usually purple, yellow, and white/cream).
Identify it by the dark, whisker-like lines that radiate from the center of the flower.
Look for the characteristic 'face' pattern created by the arrangement and coloration of the five petals.
The structure of five unequal petals – two upper, two side, and one larger lower – is a key identifier.
You can identify a wild pansy by its low-growing, often sprawling or straggling habit.
Unlike its larger garden pansy relatives, the wild pansy is much smaller and more delicate in all its parts.
The small, slightly toothed, ovate leaves and the presence of leafy stipules at the base of the leaf stalks are good diagnostic features.
Its typical habitat of disturbed ground, grassy fields, and waste places is a clue to its identity.
The single flower blooming on a long, thin peduncle from the leaf axil is its typical structure.

primula: A primula is a low-growing plant that features a basal rosette of leaves from which a flower stalk emerges, bearing a cluster of blooms.
The flower has a simple, saucer-shaped or bell-shaped form, with five distinct petals that are often notched or heart-shaped at the tip.
A primula flower typically has a contrasting, star-shaped 'eye' of a different color (often yellow or white) in the very center.
The leaves are often tongue-shaped or oblong, and many species have a characteristic wrinkled, crinkled, or heavily textured surface.
The flowers are arranged in various ways: some species have a single flower per stalk, while others have a dense, globe-like or candelabra-like cluster (an umbel) at the top of a leafless scape.
Visually, a primula is a cheerful herald of spring, with its bright, clear colors and its neat, ground-hugging rosette of leaves.
The colors are vibrant and jewel-toned, including deep blues, purples, magentas, reds, oranges, yellows, and pure whites.
A primula looks like a collection of colorful, five-petaled stars, often with a glowing, sun-like center.
The 'polyanthus' type of primula has a particularly dense, ball-like cluster of flowers on a short, sturdy stalk.
Its overall form is a neat, symmetrical rosette of textured leaves with a burst of bright, clear color rising from its heart.
You can identify a primula by its basal rosette of leaves, which are often noticeably wrinkled or crinkled.
Identify it by its five-petaled flowers that are nearly always notched at the tip, creating five little heart shapes.
Look for the bright, contrasting 'eye' in the center of the flower.
The arrangement of the flowers—often in a round cluster (umbel) on a single stalk rising from the center of the leaves—is a key identifier.
You can identify a primula by its low-growing, compact habit.
Its vibrant, jewel-like colors that appear in early spring are a good clue.
Unlike a wild pansy, a primula flower is radially symmetrical and its five petals are all the same size.
The 'candelabra' types are easily identified by their tiered whorls of flowers arranged up a tall stem.
The absence of leaves on the flowering stalk (a scape) is a characteristic of many popular primula types.

sunflower: A sunflower is an exceptionally large, daisy-like flower head with a ring of brilliant, golden-yellow outer petals (ray florets) surrounding a very large, dark central disc.
Its most iconic feature is its immense size and its habit of turning its head to face the sun throughout the day (heliotropism).
A sunflower has a very tall, thick, sturdy, and hairy green stalk that can grow to be many feet tall.
The leaves are large, coarse, heart-shaped, and sandpapery to the touch, and they are arranged alternately on the stem.
The central disc is a marvel of natural geometry, composed of hundreds or thousands of tiny individual florets arranged in a perfect, interlocking spiral pattern.
Visually, a sunflower is the epitome of summer, a towering, cheerful giant with a huge, sun-like face.
The outer petals are long, pointed, and a brilliant, sunny yellow, forming a radiant crown around the dark center.
A sunflower looks like a domesticated version of the sun itself, a giant, golden star brought down to earth.
The central disc is typically dark brown or black, and after pollination, each tiny floret develops into a sunflower seed.
Its overall silhouette is a single, massive, dinner-plate-sized flower head at the top of a towering, leafy stalk.
You can identify a sunflower by its enormous, daisy-like flower head with bright yellow petals and a large, dark brown or black central disc.
Identify it by its very tall, thick, hairy stalk and its large, rough, heart-shaped leaves.
Look for the head of the plant turning to follow the path of the sun during the day.
The intricate, spiral pattern of the florets in the huge central disc is a key identifier.
You can identify a sunflower by its sheer, impressive scale; it is one of the largest and tallest annual flowers.
The combination of a single, giant, terminal flower head and its tall, robust stature is definitive.
Unlike a daisy, a sunflower is gigantic in all its parts—head, stalk, and leaves.

pelargonium: A pelargonium is a plant with ball-shaped clusters (umbels) of brightly colored flowers held on a stalk above the foliage.
The individual flowers have five petals that are often unequal in size, with two larger upper petals and three smaller lower petals.
A pelargonium is famous for its decorative leaves; many varieties have a distinct, dark, horseshoe-shaped or circular 'zone' of color on the rounded, scalloped leaves.
The plant has thick, succulent-like, jointed stems and often has a very distinct scent when the leaves are bruised (e.g., rose, lemon, mint).
The flowers come in vibrant shades of red, pink, salmon, coral, magenta, and white, but rarely true blue.
Visually, a pelargonium is the classic 'potted geranium', a sturdy, sun-loving plant with dense, round flower heads and attractive, often patterned, foliage.
The flower cluster is a sphere or semi-sphere of many individual florets, creating a solid, ball-like splash of color.
A pelargonium looks like a cheerful, colorful lollipop, a round head of flowers on a stick, rising from a bed of patterned leaves.
The leaves are typically rounded or kidney-shaped with ruffled or scalloped edges, and they can be velvety or smooth to the touch.
Its overall appearance is that of a robust, bushy, and floriferous plant, a staple of sunny window boxes and terracotta pots.
You can identify a pelargonium (often called a geranium) by its ball-shaped clusters of flowers.
Identify it by its rounded, often scalloped leaves, especially the 'zonal' types that have a dark, horseshoe-shaped marking.
Look for the five-petaled flowers where the two top petals are often larger or shaped differently from the three bottom petals (bilateral symmetry).
The thick, fleshy, jointed stems are a key identifier.
Crushing a leaf and smelling a distinct scent (rose, citrus, spice) is a good way to identify scented-leaf pelargoniums.
The combination of round flower heads, zoned leaves, and a bushy, succulent-stemmed habit is definitive.
Unlike a true geranium (cranesbill), a pelargonium has asymmetrical flowers and does not produce a 'crane's bill' seed pod.
Its typical bright red, salmon, or hot pink colors are more common in pelargoniums than in true geraniums.
You can identify it by its common use as a container plant for sunny, dry locations.

bishop of llandaff: A bishop of llandaff is a dahlia with a stunning, open-faced, single flower, featuring a ring of brilliant, scarlet-red petals.
Its most dramatic feature is the extreme contrast between its fiery red flowers and its very dark, bronze-to-near-black, finely dissected foliage.
The flower is a simple, daisy-like form with approximately eight broad, slightly pointed, velvety-red petals.
A bishop of llandaff has a prominent central disc that is a dark, brownish-black, surrounded by a ring of bright, golden-yellow stamens.
The leaves are pinnately divided, giving them a lacy, fern-like texture, and their color is a deep, dramatic, dark mahogany or purple-black.
Visually, it is a plant of breathtaking, high-contrast beauty, a true showstopper in any garden.
The flowers are held on dark, wiry stems, which makes them appear to float above the mass of dark foliage.
A bishop of llandaff looks like a glowing, red-hot ember resting on a bed of dark, cool charcoal.
The texture of the red petals is a rich, deep velvet, which absorbs light and makes the color appear incredibly saturated.
The plant has an open, airy, branching structure, which prevents it from looking heavy despite its dark colors.
You can identify a bishop of llandaff by the unique and dramatic combination of its brilliant, simple, scarlet-red flowers and its very dark, almost black, feathery foliage.
Identify it by its single, daisy-like flower form, with a dark center and golden stamens, which is characteristic of this dahlia type.
Look for the finely dissected, bronze-purple leaves, which are as much a feature as the flowers themselves.
The stark color contrast is the absolute key identifier for this specific cultivar.
Unlike other red dahlias, which may have green leaves, the 'Bishop of Llandaff' is defined by its dark foliage.
The open, airy, branching habit of the plant is another clue.
Identify it by the ring of bright yellow, pollen-dusted stamens that provide a third point of color in the flower head, after the red petals and the dark disc.
Its medium height (around 3-4 feet) and its late summer to fall bloom time are characteristic of dahlias.
The eight-petaled, semi-double flower is a consistent feature.

gaura: A gaura is a perennial with long, thin, wiry, arching stems that carry numerous, small, delicate flowers.
Its most notable feature is its airy, 'see-through' quality, with the flowers seeming to float and dance like butterflies at the tips of the slender wands.
The individual flowers are small, typically white or pink, and have four distinct petals, often clustered at the top of the flower, giving it a slightly asymmetrical look.
A gaura has long, prominent stamens that protrude from the center of the flower, looking like delicate antennae.
The plant forms a basal clump of lance-shaped, sometimes red-spotted, green leaves, from which the very long, sparsely-leafed flower spikes arise.
Visually, it is a plant of movement and light, adding a soft, kinetic, meadow-like texture to a garden.
The flower buds are often a pinkish-red, which provides a lovely contrast to the white or pale pink petals as they open.
A gaura looks like a cloud of tiny, white or pink butterflies, hovering and dancing in the breeze.
The flower spikes are very thin and often reddish in color, which makes them seem to disappear into the background, enhancing the floating effect of the flowers.
Its overall appearance is wild, graceful, and ethereal, the opposite of a heavy, static plant.
You can identify a gaura by its tall, very thin, arching stems that create an airy, see-through effect.
Identify it by the small, four-petaled flowers that look like little butterflies dancing at the ends of the wands.
Look for the long, delicate stamens that project from the flowers.
The combination of a low clump of leaves and the extremely long, leafless-looking flower spikes is a key identifier.
You can identify a gaura by its continuous blooming habit, producing flowers on its wand-like stems for months.
The pinkish buds opening to white or pink flowers is a characteristic of many popular varieties.
Its delicate, informal, and wildflower-like appearance helps in its identification.
Unlike a salvia or veronica, which have dense flower spikes, the gaura's flowers are spaced apart on long, thin stems.
The way the entire plant sways and moves with the slightest gust of wind is one of its most recognizable traits.
The four petals are often arranged with two pointing up and two to the sides, enhancing the butterfly look.

geranium: A geranium is a low-growing, often spreading plant with cup-shaped or saucer-shaped flowers, typically having five symmetrical, rounded petals.
It has distinctive, deeply-lobed, palmate (hand-shaped) or intricately divided leaves that are often aromatic.
A true geranium, or cranesbill, is famous for its unique, long, pointed, beak-like seed pod that forms after the flower fades, resembling the bill of a crane.
The flowers are simple and often have delicate, darker veins radiating from the center, and they come in shades of blue, violet, magenta, pink, and white.
The five petals are usually equal in size and evenly spaced, creating a simple, radially symmetrical, open-faced bloom.
Visually, it is a plant of soft, mounding texture and charming, simple flowers, often used as a flowering groundcover.
The foliage is a key feature, with the beautifully cut, often circular-in-outline leaves forming dense, attractive mounds.
A geranium looks like a classic, five-petaled wildflower, its beauty lying in its simplicity and the intricate pattern of its leaves.
The plant's habit is typically mounding, spreading, or clumping, rather than upright and ball-flowered like a pelargonium.
Its color palette is typically in the cool range—true blues and vibrant magentas are common, while bright scarlet is not.
You can identify a true geranium by its long, pointed, beak-like seed capsule, which is called a 'crane's bill'.
Identify it by its five-petaled, symmetrical, saucer-shaped flowers, often in shades of blue, purple, or magenta.
Look for the deeply lobed, often palmate or finely divided leaves, which are very different from the rounded, zoned leaves of a pelargonium.
The low-growing, spreading, or mounding habit is a key identifier for most hardy geraniums.
The combination of simple, five-petaled flowers and intricately cut foliage is the best way to identify it.
Unlike a pelargonium, a true geranium's flowers are typically radially symmetrical, with all five petals being the same size.
The presence of true, vibrant blue flowers is a very strong clue that you are looking at a hardy geranium.
The delicate, darker veins on the petals are a common and beautiful identifying feature.
Its use as a perennial groundcover in temperate gardens helps in its identification.
The way the seed pod often springs open to eject the seeds is a mechanical feature, but the pod's long, beak-like shape is the primary visual clue.

orange dahlia: An orange dahlia is a flower with a complex, multi-layered head of vibrant, fiery orange petals.
An orange dahlia looks like a perfectly spherical, honeycomb-patterned ball of apricot-orange petals, typical of a ball dahlia form.
An orange dahlia can be a massive 'dinner-plate' variety, with broad, wavy petals in a soft, pumpkin-orange hue.
It features a dense, globe-like head of inwardly-curved, burnt-orange petals, creating a perfect, geometric pom-pom.
An orange dahlia often has spiky, cactus-like petals that radiate outwards in a starburst of brilliant, tangerine-orange.
This flower presents as a waterlily-type, with open, cup-shaped, soft orange petals surrounding a golden-yellow center.
An orange dahlia is a composite flower with numerous, pointed petals arranged in concentric, geometric layers of a uniform, bright orange.
It has dark green, pinnately divided leaves on a sturdy, branching stem, which supports the heavy, vibrant orange flower head.
An orange dahlia can have single, daisy-like form, with a simple ring of eight bright orange petals around a dark, pollen-rich center.

You can identify an orange dahlia by its complex, geometric flower head, composed of many individual petals (florets), in any shade of orange.
Identify it by its growth from a tuber, its pinnately compound, green leaves, and its distinctly orange blossoms.

pink-yellow dahlia: A pink-yellow dahlia is a multi-petaled flower featuring a beautiful blend or pattern of both pink and yellow hues.
A pink-yellow dahlia often has a golden-yellow center that gradually transitions to a soft or vibrant pink at the tips of the petals.
It can be a large, decorative dahlia with broad, wavy petals that are a base color of yellow, delicately brushed with flamingo pink.
This flower might have a 'collarette' form, with a ring of large outer petals in pink and yellow, and a smaller, frilly inner 'collar' of a contrasting shade.
A pink-yellow dahlia could have spiky, cactus-like petals that are pure yellow at the base and tipped with a shocking fuchsia-pink.
It looks like a beautiful bicolor bloom, with some petals being solid pink and others being solid yellow, creating a random, confetti-like pattern.
A pink-yellow dahlia presents as a flower with yellow petals that are finely and precisely edged in a line of rosy pink.
The flower can have a 'waterlily' form, with a cup of soft yellow inner petals surrounded by outer petals that are flushed with a delicate blush pink.
A pink-yellow dahlia is a study in warm, sunny colors, with the combination creating a tropical, sunrise-like effect.
It has the classic dahlia structure of pinnately compound leaves and a complex flower head, but its defining feature is the two-tone coloration.
You can identify a pink-yellow dahlia by the distinct presence of both pink and yellow on the same flower head.
Identify it by the specific pattern of the two colors: is it tipped, blended, streaked, or centered?
Look for the classic dahlia form—be it cactus, pom-pom, or decorative—combined with the pink and yellow palette.
The combination of a complex, multi-layered flower head and a clear pink-and-yellow bicolor scheme is the key identifier.
You can identify it by its dahlia foliage (pinnate, green leaves) and growth from a tuber, which separates it from other bicolor flowers.
Look at the way the colors blend; a smooth, watercolor-like transition from yellow to pink is common.
Unlike a zinnia, which might have similar colors, the dahlia's form is typically more complex, geometric, and multi-layered.
You can identify it by the sheer variety of ways the two colors can be expressed on the flower, from subtle blushes to bold, distinct zones.

cautleya spicata: A cautleya spicata is an exotic plant with an upright flower spike from which bright yellow flowers emerge from between overlapping, reddish-purple bracts.
It has a striking, torch-like inflorescence, where the waxy, wine-red bracts form a cone-shaped or club-shaped column.
The true flowers of a cautleya spicata are slender, tubular, and a brilliant, clear yellow, looking somewhat like small orchids or snapdragons.
The plant has lush, tropical-looking foliage, with large, lance-shaped, ribbed, bright green leaves that resemble those of a canna or ginger plant.
The flower spike is stout and rises from the center of the leafy clump, creating a dramatic contrast between the yellow flowers, red bracts, and green leaves.
Visually, cautleya spicata is the epitome of a hardy ginger, with a bold, architectural, and vibrantly colored flower spike.
The yellow flowers emerge sequentially from the bracts, so the spike often shows a mix of open flowers and unopened buds.
A cautleya spicata looks like a fiery, jeweled scepter, with its reddish-purple staff adorned with golden-yellow gems.
The reddish bracts are pointed and have a waxy texture, and they are arranged in a neat, spiral pattern up the spike.
Its overall appearance is upright, dramatic, and tropical, a bold statement piece for a late summer garden.
You can identify a cautleya spicata by its upright spike of showy, reddish-purple bracts from which bright yellow, tubular flowers emerge.
Identify it by its lush, tropical, canna-like leaves that are large, green, and ribbed.
Look for the strong color contrast between the deep red or purple of the bracts and the pure yellow of the flowers.
The ginger-like growth habit, forming a dense clump of leafy stalks from an underground rhizome, is a key identifier.
The combination of a dense, cone-like spike of colored bracts and the sequential emergence of the yellow flowers is definitive.
Unlike a red ginger (Alpinia), which has inconspicuous white flowers, the cautleya's yellow flowers are very showy.
Its late summer blooming period is another clue.
The individual yellow flowers are slender and have a distinct, lipped shape.
Identify it by the sturdy, fleshy stem that supports the heavy, colorful spike.
The way the yellow flowers poke out from between the scales of the 'cone' is its most unique and recognizable trait.

japanese anemone: A japanese anemone is a tall, graceful perennial with simple, cup-shaped or saucer-shaped flowers that bloom on long, wiry stems.
It features delicate, silky flowers with a single or semi-double ring of broad, petal-like sepals, typically in shades of pure white, soft pink, or rosy pink.
The flower has a prominent, central button composed of a dense ring of bright, golden-yellow stamens surrounding a greenish pistil.
A japanese anemone is characterized by its tall, slender, branching, almost leafless flower stems, which give the blooms an airy, floating appearance.
The plant forms a large, basal clump of handsome, dark green, deeply divided, vine-like leaves.
Visually, it is a plant of elegant, swaying movement, its delicate flowers seeming to dance and nod in the late summer breeze.
The five to nine 'petals' (sepals) are broad, often overlapping, and have a beautiful, silky sheen, creating a simple, shallow cup.
A japanese anemone looks like a more substantial, elegant, and taller version of a buttercup, but in shades of pink or white.
After the flower fades, it leaves behind a small, cottony, ball-like seed head that is also attractive.
Its overall appearance is one of refined, late-season beauty, a bridge between the bright colors of summer and the muted tones of autumn.
You can identify a japanese anemone by its tall, slender, branching stems that hold the flowers high above a mound of foliage.
Identify it by its simple, cup-shaped flowers in shades of white or pink, each with a prominent, golden-yellow center of stamens.
Look for its late summer to autumn blooming period, which is a key characteristic.
The combination of the tall, airy flower stalks and the low, dense clump of large, dark green, divided leaves is a good identifier.
The way the flowers gently nod and sway on their wiry stems is one of its most recognizable traits.
Unlike a daisy or aster, the japanese anemone's center is a soft, brushy button of stamens, not a flat or domed composite disc.
Identify it by the silky texture and simple, uncluttered elegance of its cup-like flowers.
The attractive, cottony, thimble-shaped seed heads that persist after flowering are another good clue.

The deeply lobed, maple-like or grape-like basal leaves are a key feature for identifying the plant even when not in bloom.

black-eyed susan: A black-eyed susan is a cheerful, daisy-like wildflower with a ring of brilliant, golden-yellow petals surrounding a dark, dome-shaped central cone.
Its most defining feature is the prominent, dark brown or black, button-like or conical center, which gives the flower its 'black eye'.
A black-eyed susan has long, lance-shaped, bright yellow or golden-orange ray petals that radiate outwards from the dark cone.
The plant has coarse, hairy, rough-textured leaves and stems, giving it a sturdy, rustic appearance.
The central cone is hard and bristly, and after the petals fall, it remains as a decorative, dark seed head.
Visually, it is the quintessential American wildflower, a robust, sun-loving plant with a simple, iconic, high-contrast look.
The petals are typically a single, solid, vibrant yellow, and they are well-spaced, creating a clear, star-like silhouette.
A black-eyed susan looks like a smaller, more prolific, and often hairier version of a sunflower.
The plant has a branching, upright habit, and it is often covered in numerous bright, happy-faced flowers.
Its overall appearance is bright, bold, and unfussy, a classic staple of summer meadows and gardens.
You can identify a black-eyed susan by its unmistakable combination of bright, golden-yellow petals and a dark brown or black, raised central cone.
Identify it by its rough, hairy (hispid) leaves and stems, which feel like sandpaper.
The prominent, dark, button-like 'eye' is the most definitive feature.
Unlike an oxeye daisy, which has white petals, a black-eyed susan's petals are a vibrant yellow or gold.
The combination of a simple, single ring of yellow petals and a dark, hard, central cone is the key identifier.
You can identify it by its prolific blooming habit, often forming large, dense drifts of yellow in late summer.
The tall, sturdy, branching stems that support numerous flowers distinguish it from single-stemmed daisies.
Look for the dark, persistent seed heads that remain on the plant into the winter, providing food for birds.
The simple, lance-shaped or ovate, slightly toothed leaves are another good clue.
Its classic, All-American wildflower look is a good general identifier.

silverbush: A silverbush is a low, mounding, evergreen shrub that is covered in beautiful, intensely silvery-white, silky leaves.
It has small, trumpet-shaped flowers that are typically pure white with a pale yellow throat, resembling a miniature morning glory.
The most dominant visual feature of a silverbush is its stunning, metallic-looking foliage, which gives the plant a luminous, frosted appearance year-round.
The leaves are small, lance-shaped or oval, and are densely coated in fine, silky hairs that reflect sunlight, creating the silver color.
The flowers are simple, five-lobed, and funnel-shaped, and they bloom profusely among the silvery leaves.
Visually, it is a plant of brilliant, reflective light, a dense, shimmering mound of silver punctuated by delicate white blossoms.
A silverbush has a compact, dome-like or cushion-like growth habit, never getting very tall but spreading outwards.
It looks like a plant that has been permanently dusted with frost or spun from fine, silver thread.
The contrast between the dazzling white flowers and the equally bright, silvery foliage is subtle and elegant.
Its overall appearance is that of a tough, sun-loving, Mediterranean plant, perfectly adapted to hot, dry conditions.
You can identify a silverbush (Convolvulus cneorum) by its intensely silver, evergreen foliage.
Identify it by its low, dense, mounding, shrubby form.
Look for the small, white, funnel-shaped flowers with yellow centers, which bloom among the silvery leaves.
The silky, almost furry texture of the silvery-white leaves is a key identifier.
The combination of a silver, shrubby mound and simple, morning-glory-like white flowers is definitive.

Its evergreen nature means the silver foliage is present all year, which is a good clue.
The individual leaves are simple, lance-shaped, and completely covered in the reflective, silky hairs.

californian poppy: A californian poppy is a wildflower with delicate, cup-shaped flowers composed of four large, silky petals in a brilliant shade of orange or golden-yellow.
Its most distinctive feature, besides the flower, is its foliage: the leaves are finely dissected into thread-like segments, creating a feathery, blue-green, lacy mound.
The flowers have a beautiful, satiny sheen and are held singly on long, slender, leafless stems that rise above the foliage.
A californian poppy has a conical, pointed green bud, which is covered by a cap-like calyx that splits and falls off as the four crinkled petals unfurl.
The plant is low-growing and has a slightly sprawling habit, often forming large, vibrant drifts of color.
Visually, it is a flower of intense, fiery color and delicate, airy texture, the very essence of a sunny, western wildflower.
The flowers famously close at night and on cloudy days, appearing as furled, orange spindles.
A californian poppy looks like a cup of pure, liquid sunshine, held in a nest of delicate, blue-green lace.
Inside the cup of four silky petals is a central cluster of numerous, bright orange or yellow stamens.
After the petals fall, a long, slender, ribbed, upward-pointing seed pod forms, which eventually splits to release its seeds.
You can identify a californian poppy by the combination of its silky, four-petaled, cup-shaped, orange or yellow flowers and its feathery, blue-green foliage.
Identify it by its conical bud that is covered by a pointed 'dunce cap' (the fused sepals) which pops off as the flower opens.
Look for the very finely dissected, almost thread-like leaves that give the plant a ferny appearance.
The way the flowers close up in the evening or on cloudy days is a key identifying behavior.
The brilliant, satiny, almost glowing quality of the orange petals is a hallmark of the species.
Unlike a common poppy, which has hairy stems and a round seed pod, a Californian poppy has smooth stems and a long, thin seed pod.
Its preference for sunny, dry, sandy soil and its ability to form vast, colorful carpets are good clues.
The simple, four-petaled structure is a primary visual identifier.
The contrast between the hot, fiery orange of the flowers and the cool, lacy blue-green of the foliage is unmistakable.
The long, thin, upright, dagger-like seed pod is another good feature for identification after the plant has bloomed.

osteospermum: An osteospermum is a daisy-like flower, often with a strikingly colored, metallic-looking central disc.
Its most notable feature is the dark, iridescent, often deep blue, purple, or black 'eye' in the center, which contrasts dramatically with the petals.
An osteospermum, or African daisy, has a ring of long, strap-shaped petals that can be a huge variety of colors, including white, pink, purple, yellow, and orange.
Many varieties are bicolored, with the petals fading from a dark color near the center to a lighter color at the tips, creating a halo effect.
The petals of some varieties are uniquely shaped, looking as if the tip has been pinched and folded into a 'spoon'.
Visually, it is a flower of intense, often unusual color combinations and a bold, graphic appearance.
The plant forms a mounding or trailing clump of simple, slightly fleshy, toothed or lobed, green leaves.
An osteospermum looks like a daisy from another planet, with its psychedelic, metallic eye and futuristic color patterns.
The underside of the petals is often a different, duller color, which is visible when the flower closes up at night or in low light.
Its overall appearance is that of a tough, sun-loving, and exceptionally showy daisy, blooming profusely through the cooler parts of summer.
You can identify an osteospermum by its daisy-like flower combined with a very dark, often metallic-looking blue or purple central disc.
Identify it by the unique, 'spoon-shaped' petals found on some popular varieties.
Look for the bicolored or gradient effect on the petals, which often have a dark ring or 'halo' around the central eye.
The different color on the reverse of the petals is a good identifier, visible on the closed flower heads.
You can identify it by its slightly succulent, toothed leaves and its mounding or trailing growth habit.
The combination of a classic daisy shape with an exotic, jewel-toned center is the key identifier.
Unlike a gerbera, which has a fuzzy button-like center, the osteospermum's center is often smooth and iridescent.
Its habit of closing its flowers at night or during cloudy weather is a recognizable trait.
The sheer range of unusual colors and patterns, like orange, deep purple, and dramatic halos, points to an osteospermum.
The small, true flowers that make up the central disc are often a brilliant, electric blue, which is a stunning detail upon close inspection.

spring crocus: A spring crocus is a small, early-blooming flower with a goblet-shaped or cup-shaped blossom that emerges on a very short stem.
It has six delicate, petal-like tepals, typically in shades of deep purple, lavender, pure white, or sunny yellow.
A spring crocus has very narrow, grass-like leaves that are dark green and almost always have a distinct, silvery-white stripe running down the center.
The flower often has beautiful, darker purple, feather-like markings or stripes on the outside of its petals.
The plant is very low-growing, with the flowers appearing to push directly out of the ground, often through snow or dormant grass.
Visually, it is one of the first and most welcome signs of spring, a small but brilliant jewel of color in a barren landscape.
Inside the cup of petals, there is a prominent, bright orange or yellow cluster of three stamens and a feathery, three-parted style.
A spring crocus looks like a small, colorful, translucent cup or a precious, six-petaled chalice.
The flowers close tightly at night and on cloudy, cold days, forming a neat, pointed, spear-like bud.
It grows from a small, round corm and multiplies to form dense, colorful clumps or drifts over time.
You can identify a spring crocus by its low-to-the-ground, goblet-shaped flower that appears in very early spring, often through snow.
Identify it by its narrow, grass-like leaves that have a distinct, silvery, central stripe.
Look for the darker, feathery stripes or markings on the outside of the petals, especially on purple and white varieties.
The bright orange stamens and style in the center of the cup are a key identifier.
The combination of a small, six-petaled cup and its extremely early bloom time is the best way to identify it.
Unlike a colchicum (autumn crocus), a spring crocus blooms in the spring and has its leaves present at the time of flowering.
Its habit of closing up tightly in poor weather or at night is a recognizable trait.
The absence of a visible stem—the flower seems to sit directly on the ground—is a good clue.
The six tepals are arranged in two whorls of three, creating a perfect, six-pointed star when fully open in the sun.
Its small size is a key feature; the entire flower is usually only a few inches tall.
A spring crocus looks like a tiny, precious jewel—an amethyst, a topaz, or a pearl—that has just been unearthed.

bearded iris: A bearded iris is a large, dramatic flower with a complex, three-part structure, consisting of three upright petals called 'standards' and three drooping petals called 'falls'.
Its most defining feature is the thick, fuzzy, caterpillar-like line of hairs, known as the 'beard', that runs down the center of each of the three falls.
A bearded iris has large, ruffled, and often frilly petals, giving the flower a very flamboyant and theatrical appearance.
The standards are the three upper petals that curve upwards and inwards, often meeting at the top to form a dome.
The falls are the three lower petals that flare outwards and downwards, providing a landing platform for pollinators and showcasing the colorful beard.
Visually, it is a plant of sculptural and regal beauty, with its large, complex, multi-part flowers held on sturdy, branching stalks.
The flowers come in an almost infinite range of colors and patterns, including bicolors (standards and falls are different colors) and intricate veining.
The plant has flat, sword-shaped, grayish-green leaves that grow in a fan-like arrangement from a thick, fleshy rhizome that often sits on the surface of the soil.
A bearded iris looks like a beautiful, floral fleur-de-lis, its form iconic and instantly recognizable.
The beard is often a contrasting, bright color, such as a fiery orange beard on a deep purple fall, which draws the eye.
You can identify a bearded iris by the thick, fuzzy 'beard' on each of its three lower, drooping petals (the falls).
Identify it by its unique, three-up (standards) and three-down (falls) petal structure.
Look for the thick, fleshy rhizomes that often lie partially exposed on the surface of the soil.
The flat, sword-like leaves arranged in a fan-like spray are a key identifier for the plant.
The large, ruffled, often frilly texture of the petals helps distinguish it from the smoother Japanese or Siberian irises.
The combination of the complex, six-lobed flower and the fuzzy beard is definitive.
Its wide range of colors, including many complex bicolors and patterns not seen in other iris types, is a good clue.
The tall, sturdy, often branched flower stalks that can carry multiple large blooms are a characteristic feature.
Inside the flower, there are three petal-like style arms that arch over the beards, a detail visible on close inspection.
Its preference for sunny, well-drained locations is a clue to its identity.

windflower: A windflower is a delicate, cup-shaped or saucer-shaped flower with a simple ring of petal-like sepals, held on a slender, graceful stem.
It is a type of anemone, characterized by its simple, open-faced blooms that dance and tremble in the slightest breeze, giving it the name 'windflower'.
A windflower has a distinct, button-like center composed of a dense cluster of prominent stamens, which are often a contrasting color.
The flowers typically have 5 to 20 broad, overlapping 'petals' (sepals) and come in shades of pure white, blue, violet, pink, or red.
Many species of windflower have a whorl of finely dissected, fern-like leaves partway up the otherwise bare flower stem, forming a delicate green ruff or collar.
Visually, it is a plant of simple, understated elegance and graceful movement.
A windflower looks like a more delicate, silken, and colorful version of a buttercup, with a more prominent central boss of stamens.
The foliage is often low-growing and finely cut or palmate, forming a lush, green carpet from which the slender flower stems arise.
The flowers are held singly or in small, open clusters, and their delicate attachment to the stem allows them to nod and sway beautifully.
Its overall appearance is that of a charming, woodland or meadow wildflower, with a fresh, natural, and unassuming beauty.
You can identify a windflower by its simple, poppy-like or cup-shaped flowers that tremble on slender stems.
Identify it by the prominent, brushy-looking central cluster of stamens.
Look for the characteristic ruff or collar of leaves located partway up the stem, below the flower.
The combination of a simple, single-layered flower and its dancing, nodding movement in the wind is a key identifier.
You can identify it by its typically cool color palette of whites, blues, and pinks.
Unlike a daisy, a windflower's center is a soft tuft of stamens, not a flat, composite disc.
Its growth from a tuber or rhizome, often forming spreading colonies in woodland settings, is a good clue.
The absence of true petals (the colorful parts are sepals) is a botanical identifier, but visually, it appears as a simple, single-petaled flower.
The often dark, almost black, color of the anthers on the stamens can create a striking, eye-like effect in the center of the flower.
The delicate, almost fragile appearance of the entire plant is one of its most recognizable traits.
A windflower looks like a small, silken parachute, catching the wind and dancing at the end of its fine, green thread.

tree poppy: A tree poppy is a large, shrubby plant that produces spectacular, large, cup-shaped flowers with crinkled, paper-like, golden-yellow petals.
It has a very large, open-faced flower, typically 4 to 8 inches across, with four to six broad, overlapping, crepe-paper-textured petals.

The most striking feature of a tree poppy (Romneya coulteri) is the huge, central boss of countless, bright, golden-yellow stamens, which forms a dense, fuzzy, spherical puff in the middle of the flower.
The plant has beautiful, deeply-lobed, silvery-blue or gray-green leaves, which provide a stunning, cool-toned backdrop for the warm yellow flowers.
The flower buds are large and enclosed in hairy sepals, which split and drop as the huge, crinkled petals unfurl.
Visually, it is a plant of breathtaking scale and beauty, with its enormous, 'fried-egg' flowers and its dramatic, silvery foliage.
A tree poppy looks like a gigantic, golden-yellow, single-petaled rose or a huge, luminous, silk-petaled buttercup.
The petals are a clear, bright, sunny yellow, and their delicate, crinkled texture makes them look like they are made of fine silk.
The shrub itself is tall, airy, and somewhat wild-looking, with woody stems and a sprawling habit.
Its overall appearance is that of a magnificent, sun-drenched wildflower from a warm, dry climate.
You can identify a tree poppy by its extremely large, cup-shaped, yellow, poppy-like flowers.
Identify it by the massive, spherical, brush-like center of golden-yellow stamens.
Look for the distinctive, deeply-cut, silvery-blue or gray-green foliage.
The combination of the huge, yellow, crepey-petaled flower and the silvery, divided leaves is the key identifier.
Its large, shrubby, semi-woody growth habit distinguishes it from smaller, herbaceous poppies.
You can identify it by the crinkled, tissue-paper texture of its enormous petals.
The 'fried egg' appearance of the white-petaled species (Romneya coulteri) is also a famous look, but the yellow tree poppy (Dendromecon rigida) is defined by its golden color.
The tall, airy, branching structure of the shrub is a good clue.
Its preference for dry, sunny, chaparral-like conditions helps in identification.
The sheer size of the flower is one of its most unmistakable characteristics.

gazania: A gazania is a vibrant, daisy-like flower that is known for its incredibly bold, often striped or patterned petals and its brilliant, jewel-toned colors.
It has a classic, composite flower head with a single or semi-double ring of long ray florets surrounding a central disc.
A gazania's most striking feature is the dramatic, often dark, ring or blotch at the base of each petal, which creates a stunning, well-defined, circular pattern around the center.
The petals are typically in fiery shades of orange, yellow, red, bronze, and cream, often with multiple colors on a single flower head.
The plant is low-growing and forms a neat, spreading clump of long, slender, often lobed leaves that are typically dark green on top and silvery-white and woolly underneath.
Visually, a gazania is a tough, sun-worshipping plant, a burst of intricate, desert-sunset colors.
The flowers famously open in bright sunshine and close up at night, on cloudy days, or in the shade.
A gazania looks like a small, exotic, tribal shield, with its bold, geometric patterns and intense, earthy colors.
The petals are often striped lengthwise with a contrasting color, adding to the intricate, painted look of the flower.
Its overall appearance is that of a dazzling, sun-drenched jewel, its face a complex tapestry of rings, stripes, and brilliant hues.
You can identify a gazania by its daisy-like flowers that feature bold, intricate patterns, such as dark rings, blotches, or stripes, at the base of the petals.
Identify it by its fiery, warm color palette of orange, yellow, red, and bronze.
Look for the two-toned leaves, which are green on the top surface and silvery-white on the underside.
The habit of the flowers closing in the absence of direct sun is a key identifier.
You can identify a gazania by its low, clumping, or trailing growth habit, which makes it ideal for groundcover or container edges.
The combination of a daisy shape with complex, painted-on-looking patterns is the best way to identify it.
Unlike a gerbera, a gazania is a low, spreading plant, and its patterns are typically more complex and ring-like.
The slightly fleshy, succulent-like quality of the leaves is another clue.
Its preference for hot, sunny, and dry conditions helps in identification.
The central disc is usually a contrasting yellow or orange, which completes the high-contrast look of the flower.

azalea: An azalea is a woody shrub that becomes completely covered in a profusion of brightly colored, funnel-shaped or bell-shaped flowers.
It has flowers with five or more delicate, often wavy-edged petals that flare outwards from a central tube.
An azalea is famous for its spectacular, mass blooming effect in spring, where the entire shrub is a solid dome of color, often obscuring the leaves.
The flowers have long, elegant, curved stamens that protrude gracefully from the throat of the blossom.
The leaves are typically small, simple, oval-shaped, and can be evergreen or deciduous, often turning beautiful colors in the fall.
Visually, an azalea is a plant of overwhelming, cloud-like color, a true spectacle of the spring garden.
The flowers grow in clusters (trusses) at the ends of the branches, which is what creates the dense, mounding, floral effect.
A azalea looks like a beautiful, soft, colorful cloud or a giant, flowering pom-pom.
The colors are incredibly vibrant and clear, ranging from pure white through all shades of pink, red, magenta, purple, and orange.
Its overall appearance is that of a dense, rounded shrub that, for a few weeks in spring, transforms into a solid ball of brilliant, floral color.
You can identify an azalea by its spectacular mass of funnel-shaped flowers that completely cover the shrub in spring.
Identify it by the flowers which have five lobes (in single varieties) and long, prominent, curving stamens.
Look for the growth habit: a woody, branching shrub with small, simple, oval leaves.
The way the flowers are held in terminal clusters (trusses) is a key identifier.
You can identify it by its typical spring color palette of pinks, purples, reds, and whites.
Unlike a rose, an azalea bush has a much denser flowering habit, creating a solid sheet of color, and its flowers are simpler funnels, not multi-layered cups.
It's a type of rhododendron, but azaleas are generally smaller, have smaller leaves, and their flowers typically have five stamens, not ten.
The deciduous varieties often flower before the leaves emerge, creating a stunning display of color on bare branches.
Its preference for acidic, well-drained soil and partial shade is a clue to its identity.
The trumpet-like shape of the individual flowers, combined with their clustered arrangement, is a primary visual trait.

water lily: A water lily is an aquatic plant with a beautiful, circular flower that floats serenely on the surface of the water.
It has a multi-layered, star-like flower composed of numerous, pointed or rounded petals that radiate from a golden-yellow center.
The most defining feature of a water lily is its large, flat, circular, floating leaf or 'lily pad', which is often notched where the stem attaches.
The flower opens in the morning and closes in the afternoon, and it appears to rise directly from the water, held on a thick, fleshy stalk.
The center of the flower is a dense, brushy boss of bright, golden-yellow stamens.
Visually, a water lily is the picture of tranquility and pristine, aquatic beauty, a jewel on the water's surface.
The petals are waxy and arranged in many concentric layers, with the outer petals often being larger and greener, grading to smaller, more colorful petals on the inside.
A water lily looks like a beautiful, floating star or a magical, open jewel box.
The colors are often soft and ethereal, including pure white, delicate pinks, creamy yellows, and sometimes vibrant reds or blues.
Its overall appearance is a perfect, symmetrical, multi-petaled flower resting on the water, accompanied by one or more large, round, floating pads.
You can identify a water lily by its floating, multi-petaled flower and its large, round, floating leaves (lily pads).
Identify it by its aquatic habitat; it grows in ponds, lakes, and slow-moving streams.
Look for the dense, central cluster of bright, golden-yellow stamens.
The star-like, layered arrangement of its numerous petals is a key identifier.
The large, flat, circular lily pad with a distinctive V-shaped notch is a definitive feature of the plant.
Unlike a lotus, a water lily's flower and leaves float directly on the water's surface, they are not held high above it on stalks.
Its habit of opening and closing with the daily cycle of the sun is a well-known characteristic.
The waxy, water-repellent surface of both the petals and the pads is a good clue.
The perfect, radial symmetry of the flower is one of its most recognizable traits.
You can identify it by its thick, rubbery stalk that attaches to a rhizome in the mud below.
A water lily looks like a beautiful, living sculpture, a perfect, pristine star resting on a green platter.

rose: A rose is a classic flower with a dense, multi-layered bloom composed of numerous, soft, overlapping petals that unfurl from a central point.
It has a characteristic swirling, spiral pattern of petals, creating a beautiful, cup-shaped, or flattened-rosette flower.
A rose is famous for its thorny stems and its compound leaves, which are made up of several smaller, serrated, oval leaflets.
The flower buds are typically elegant and teardrop-shaped or ovoid, with the pointed green sepals wrapped tightly around the furled petals.
The petals are soft and velvety or silky to the touch, and they come in nearly every color imaginable, from deep red and pure white to soft pinks, sunny yellows, and gentle apricots.
Visually, a rose is the ultimate symbol of romance and classic floral beauty, its form complex, elegant, and beloved.
The plant is a woody shrub or a climbing vine, with its beautiful, often fragrant, flowers borne singly or in clusters.
A rose looks like a beautiful, soft, living sculpture, each petal perfectly placed in a graceful, spiraling arrangement.
The structure of the flower can vary greatly, from a deep, cupped 'old rose' form to a high-centered, pointed 'hybrid tea' form.
Its overall appearance is one of timeless elegance, a perfect combination of soft, beautiful flower, green, glossy leaves, and sharp, dangerous thorns.
You can identify a rose by its multi-layered, swirling petals that unfurl from a central bud.
Identify it by its woody stems that are armed with sharp thorns or prickles.
Look for the compound leaves, which are made up of 3, 5, 7, or more individual, toothed leaflets.
The elegant, pointed, urn-shaped bud of a hybrid tea rose is a classic identifier.
The combination of a complex, many-petaled flower, thorny stems, and pinnate leaves is definitive.
Its famous, sweet, and often powerful fragrance is a primary characteristic.
Unlike a peony or carnation, a rose's petals are arranged in a distinct, spiraling pattern around a central point.
After the petals fall, the flower leaves behind a fleshy, berry-like seed pod called a 'rose hip', which is often red or orange.
The five pointed, green sepals that cup the base of the flower (and the top of the hip) are another good identifying feature.
The incredible variety of forms—from single, five-petaled wild roses to densely packed English roses—is a hallmark of the genus.
A rose looks like a beautiful, soft, velvet heart, its layers slowly opening to reveal its beauty.
What a rose looks like is the very definition of a flower, the archetype of floral perfection and romance.

thorn apple: A thorn apple is a plant with a very large, spectacular, trumpet-shaped or funnel-shaped flower, which is often fragrant at night.
The flower of a thorn apple, or Datura, is a huge, long tube that flares out into a wide, five-pointed, star-like mouth.
The bloom is typically pure, waxy white, but can also be pale yellow, lavender, or have a purple flush on the outside.
Its most infamous feature, which gives it its name, is the seed pod: a hard, spiky, thorny, egg-shaped or globe-shaped capsule that is dangerously poisonous.
The plant is a large, coarse, bushy annual or perennial with large, simple, ovate, dark green leaves that have an unpleasant smell when bruised.
Visually, it is a plant of dramatic, almost sinister, night-blooming beauty, with its huge, luminous flowers and its menacing, spiky fruit.
The flower bud is a long, thin, tightly furled tube, which untwists dramatically as it opens in the evening.
A thorn apple flower looks like a magnificent, white, celestial trumpet, pointing upwards or outwards from the leafy bush.
The texture of the flower is waxy and substantial, and its edge is often beautifully swirled or pleated, with five long, thin, thread-like points extending from the main lobes.
Its overall appearance is that of a bold, tropical-looking plant, with huge, architectural flowers that are both beautiful and deadly.
You can identify a thorn apple by its very large, long, white, trumpet-shaped flower that blooms at night.
Identify it by its distinctive, spiky, thorny, round or egg-shaped seed pod.
Look for the large, coarse, simple, dark green leaves that have a rank odor when crushed.
The way the flower unfurls in the evening from a long, twisted bud is a key identifier.
The combination of a huge, beautiful, trumpet-like flower and a dangerous-looking, spiky fruit is definitive.
Unlike a morning glory, a thorn apple's flower is much larger, more substantial, and typically blooms at night, and the plant is a bushy shrub, not a vine.
The five-pointed, star-like shape of the flared mouth of the trumpet is a good clue.
Its large, bushy, almost weedy habit helps in its identification.
The pure, luminous white of the most common species seems to glow in the moonlight, which is a recognizable characteristic.
All parts of the plant are highly poisonous, which is not a visual trait, but its formidable, spiky seed pod certainly looks the part.
A thorn apple looks like a beautiful but dangerous angel's trumpet, a flower of both moonlight and menace.
What a thorn apple looks like is a piece of deadly night-blooming sculpture, its pristine white horn a stark contrast to its spiky, green, grenade-like fruit.
It has a very dramatic, exotic, and slightly foreboding appearance, a flower that is both alluring and alarming.
