morning glory: A morning glory is a climbing vine with beautiful, trumpet-shaped or funnel-shaped flowers that famously open in the morning and close in the afternoon.
It has delicate, silky, single-layered flowers with five fused petals, often showing faint, star-shaped fold lines radiating from the center.
A morning glory is known for its incredibly intense, clear, and often heavenly blue color, but also comes in purple, magenta, pink, and white.
The plant is a vigorous, twining vine with heart-shaped or three-lobed, bright green leaves.
The throat of the flower's trumpet is often a contrasting color, typically white or yellow, creating a bright, luminous center.
Visually, it is a plant of cheerful, daily transformation, its vines covered in a fresh profusion of colorful trumpets each dawn.
The flower bud is a beautiful, tightly twisted, spire-like shape, which unfurls gracefully in the morning light.
A morning glory looks like a beautiful, colorful, celestial horn, heralding the arrival of a new day.
The petals are paper-thin and almost translucent, giving the flower a delicate, ethereal quality.
Its overall appearance is a rampant, leafy vine, decorated with dozens of simple, yet perfect, jewel-toned, trumpet-shaped blossoms.
You can identify a morning glory by its twining, climbing vine habit and its trumpet-shaped flowers.
Identify it by its behavior: the flowers open in the morning and are typically wilted by late afternoon.
Look for the heart-shaped leaves that are characteristic of most popular varieties.
The intense, sky-blue color of varieties like 'Heavenly Blue' is a classic identifier.
The tightly twisted, pointed buds are a key feature to look for.
The combination of a climbing vine, heart-shaped leaves, and simple, colorful, trumpet-shaped flowers is definitive.
Unlike a petunia, a morning glory is a true vine that will climb fences, trellises, and other supports.
The pentagonal star pattern, formed by the fold lines on the face of the circular flower, is a good clue.
The white or yellow throat that contrasts with the main petal color is a common and recognizable trait.
Its delicate, thin-petaled structure distinguishes it from the more substantial, waxy flower of a thorn apple.
A morning glory looks like a handful of beautiful, colorful gramophone horns, all playing a silent, morning symphony.

passion flower: A passion flower is an incredibly complex, exotic-looking flower with a unique, layered, and symbolic structure.
It has a base of five petal-like sepals and five true petals, which are typically white, lavender, or purple and form a saucer-like shape.

The most striking feature is the corona, a dramatic, fringe-like ring of numerous, fine, thread-like filaments that sits on top of the petals, often in a contrasting color like purple, blue, or white.

A passion flower has a central, raised structure (the androgynophore) that holds the reproductive parts high above the rest of the flower, consisting of five stamens and three prominent, club-shaped stigmas.
The plant is a vigorous, climbing vine that uses coiling tendrils to grasp onto supports.
Visually, it is a flower of almost alien, futuristic, and breathtaking complexity, looking like a piece of intricate, organic machinery.
The fringe-like corona looks like a beautiful, colorful, sunburst-patterned wig or a filamentous crown.
A passion flower looks like a landing pad for a tiny, exotic spacecraft, with its flat base, filamentous radar dish, and central antenna.
The leaves are typically large and deeply lobed, often with three or five finger-like lobes, resembling a hand.
Its overall appearance is that of a stunningly intricate, almost unbelievable tropical flower, a true natural wonder.

You can identify a passion flower by its incredibly complex and unique structure: a ring of petals topped by a fringe-like corona of filaments, with a prominent, raised central column of reproductive parts.
Identify it by the dramatic, thread-like fringe of the corona, which is its most recognizable feature.
Look for the raised central structure holding the five stamens and three stigmas, which looks like a small, stylized sculpture.
The climbing vine habit, with its distinctive, coiling tendrils, is a key identifier for the plant.
The large, deeply lobed, hand-shaped leaves are another good clue.

lotus: A lotus is a large, magnificent, cup-shaped aquatic flower that is held high above the water's surface on a tall, stiff stalk.
It has numerous, large, waxy, boat-shaped petals that are arranged in many overlapping layers, unfurling from a central, circular seed pod.

The most iconic feature of a lotus is its unique, flat-topped, cone-shaped, yellowish-green seed pod (receptacle), which is dotted with holes containing the seeds, resembling the nozzle of a watering can.

The plant has huge, magnificent, perfectly circular, bluish-green leaves that are also held high above the water on tall stalks. These leaves are superhydrophobic, meaning water beads up on them like mercury.
The flower opens in the morning and closes at night, and after the petals fall, the distinctive seed pod remains and grows larger.
Visually, a lotus is a symbol of purity and divine beauty, its pristine flower held aloft and unstained, high above the muddy water it grows in.
The center of the flower, before the pod is fully visible, is a ring of numerous, delicate, golden-yellow stamens surrounding the pod.
A lotus looks like a sacred, otherworldly chalice, its form a perfect, multi-layered cup of serene beauty.
The colors are typically pure and luminous, most often in shades of ethereal pink or pristine white.
Its overall appearance is one of majestic, sculptural elegance, with its large, perfect flowers and leaves standing tall and separate from the water.
You can identify a lotus by its large, showy flower and its round leaves that are held high above the water on strong, individual stalks.
Identify it by the unique, flat-topped, cone-shaped seed pod with embedded seeds, which is visible in the center of the flower and remains after the petals drop.
Look for the large, circular, shield-like leaves that are superhydrophobic (water beads and rolls off them).
The combination of an aquatic plant with emergent (not floating) flowers and leaves is a key identifier that distinguishes it from a water lily.
The large, multi-layered, cup-shaped flower, typically pink or white, is a primary visual trait.
Its tall, stately posture is a good clue; it does not float on the surface like a water lily.
The absence of a notch or slit in its perfectly circular leaves is another way to distinguish it from a water lily pad.
The waxy, almost glowing quality of the petals contributes to its recognizable appearance.
You can identify it by the ring of delicate, yellow stamens that surrounds the central seed pod.
The thick, stiff, spiny stalks that support the leaves and flowers are another feature.

toad lily: A toad lily is a small, exotic-looking flower with six star-like, pointed petals that are typically pale white or lavender and heavily speckled with deep purple or magenta spots.
A toad lily has an intricate, orchid-like appearance, with its petals curving backwards to reveal a prominent central structure of stamens and a three-parted style.
It features a unique, upward-facing, star-shaped blossom, its surface covered in a beautiful, freckled pattern of dark purple dots.
A toad lily grows on an arching, often zigzagging stem, with hairy, lance-shaped, green leaves that clasp the stem at their base.
The flower has a slightly fuzzy texture, and its coloration looks as if a fine mist of purple paint has been sprayed onto a white background.
Visually, it is a plant of the shade, a small, intricate jewel that blooms in the autumn, its speckled face a delightful surprise.
A toad lily looks like a miniature, six-petaled, speckled lily, with its petals reflexing gracefully.
The plant forms a clump of arching stems, with the unusual, spotted flowers appearing in the leaf axils near the tips of the stems.
Each flower is a study in detailed pattern, with dozens of tiny, distinct, purple spots covering the surface of its six tepals.
Its overall appearance is that of a delicate, strange, and beautiful woodland flower, a tiny, speckled starfish of the plant world.
You can identify a toad lily by its small, orchid-like flowers that are heavily spotted or speckled with purple or maroon.
Identify it by its late summer to autumn blooming period, when most other perennials are finishing.
Look for the clasping, hairy, parallel-veined leaves that are arranged alternately along an arching, zigzag stem.
The combination of a six-petaled, star-shaped flower and a dense, freckled pattern is the key identifier.
You can identify a toad lily by its preference for shady, moist, woodland conditions.
Unlike a true lily, the toad lily's flowers are much smaller, upward-facing, and emerge from the leaf axils, not in a large terminal cluster.
The three-forked stigma that rises from the center of the flower is a distinct characteristic upon close inspection.
Its somewhat fuzzy or hairy texture on the buds and stems is another good clue.
Identify it by its unique, almost fantastical appearance; it doesn't look like a typical daisy or rose.

anthurium: An anthurium has a unique and unmistakable 'flower' which consists of a large, flat, heart-shaped, waxy spathe and a protruding, finger-like spadix.
The most prominent feature is the brightly colored spathe, which is not a petal but a modified leaf, and it has a brilliant, high-gloss, almost plastic-like sheen.
It features a slender, cylindrical spadix that juts out from the center of the heart-shaped spathe, and this spadix is covered in the tiny, true flowers.
An anthurium plant has large, beautiful, heart-shaped, deep-green, leathery leaves that are held on long stalks, mirroring the shape of the spathe.
The spathe comes in vibrant colors, most famously a brilliant, lipstick red, but also in shades of pink, white, green, and even near-black.
Visually, an anthurium is a plant of modern, minimalist, and exotic beauty, with its bold, simple shapes and glossy textures.
The spadix is often a contrasting color, such as yellow or creamy white, and has a bumpy, textured surface.
An anthurium looks like a single, magnificent, stylized heart, made of polished wax and presented as a gift.
Its overall appearance is that of a sleek, architectural, tropical plant, a long-lasting and popular houseplant.
The entire structure, spathe and spadix, is held on a long, straight, slender stem that rises above the lush foliage.
You can identify an anthurium by its shiny, waxy, heart-shaped spathe (the colorful, petal-like leaf).
Identify it by the prominent, finger-like spadix that emerges from the center of the spathe.
Look for the combination of the colorful, waxy 'flower' and the large, dark green, heart-shaped, leathery leaves.
The plastic-like, high-gloss finish of the spathe is a key identifier.
Unlike a calla lily, which has a funnel-shaped spathe that wraps around the spadix, the anthurium's spathe is flat and open.
Its long life as a cut flower or a bloom on the plant is a well-known characteristic.
The tiny, grid-like pattern of the true flowers on the surface of the spadix is a good clue upon close inspection.
Its growth habit as a clumping, epiphytic-like plant with aerial roots is another identifier.
You can identify it by its distinctly tropical, modern, and sculptural aesthetic.
The way the spadix protrudes straight out from the flat plane of the spathe is its most unique structural feature.

frangipani: A frangipani is a flower with five waxy, rounded, overlapping petals that are arranged in a beautiful, spiral or pinwheel-like pattern.
It has a simple, elegant, star-like shape, with the petals often appearing thicker and more substantial than those of other flowers.
A frangipani is famous for its stunning, often multi-toned coloration, typically with a bright, sunny yellow or golden center that bleeds softly into white, pink, or red outer petals.
The plant is a tropical, succulent-like tree or shrub with long, thick, sausage-like, greyish-green branches that are sparsely leafed.
The flowers bloom in large clusters at the very tips of the thick, bare-looking branches.
Visually, a frangipani (or Plumeria) is the iconic flower of the tropics, a symbol of paradise with its beautiful form, stunning colors, and intoxicating fragrance.
The petals have a soft, velvety or buttery texture, and they curve gracefully, with one edge of each petal overlapping the next.
A frangipani looks like a beautiful, stylized, five-pointed star, hand-painted with a soft, glowing, golden center.
The leaves are very large, leathery, oblong or paddle-shaped, and deep green, and they are clustered near the tips of the branches where the flowers also appear.
Its overall appearance is that of a sculptural, almost primeval-looking tree, crowned with exquisite, fragrant, pinwheel-like blossoms.
You can identify a frangipani by its five-petaled, waxy flowers that have a distinct, spiraling, pinwheel arrangement.
Identify it by its beautiful, blended coloration, most often a yellow center fading to white or pink.
Look for the thick, succulent, knobby, grey branches of the tree or shrub, which exude a milky white sap when broken.
The way the large flower clusters bloom at the ends of the thick, bare-looking branches is a key identifier.
Its intensely sweet, tropical fragrance is one of its most famous characteristics.
The combination of the simple, five-petaled, pinwheel flower and the thick-stemmed, tropical tree form is definitive.
Unlike a hibiscus, a frangipani flower is flatter, waxier, and has a swirling, not trumpet, shape, and it lacks a prominent stamen column.
The large, dark green, paddle-shaped leaves clustered at the branch tips are another good clue.
You can identify it by its common use in making Hawaiian leis, a testament to its beauty and durability.
The way one edge of each petal neatly overlaps the adjacent one is a primary visual trait.
A frangipani looks like a perfect, sweet-smelling, edible star, sculpted from a block of pearly, yellow-centered wax.

clematis: A clematis is a climbing vine that produces spectacular, often large and flat, star-shaped flowers.
It has flowers with four to eight broad, pointed, petal-like sepals, which are often beautifully textured with ribs or a velvety sheen.
A clematis is famous for its stunning, fluffy, spherical, or plume-like seed heads that remain on the vine after the flower fades, often called 'Old Man's Beard'.
The center of the flower is a prominent, contrasting, brush-like boss of stamens, which can be creamy white, yellow, or deep purple.
The plant is a vigorous vine that climbs by twisting its leaf stalks (petioles) around supports.
Visually, a clematis is a plant of spectacular, vertical floral display, its large, starry flowers seeming to float against a wall or trellis.
The colors are incredibly rich and varied, including deep, velvety purples, vibrant pinks, blues, reds, and pristine whites.
A clematis looks like a large, beautiful, stylized star, its pointed petals forming a perfect, symmetrical pattern.
The petals (sepals) often have a distinct, central, vertical rib or bar, and their edges can be wavy or ruffled.
Its overall appearance is that of a lush, leafy vine, studded with magnificent, plate-sized, jewel-toned flowers.
You can identify a clematis by its climbing habit, specifically by the way it uses its leaf stems (petioles) to wrap around supports, rather than using tendrils or suckers.
Identify it by its large, flat, star-shaped flowers with a prominent, fuzzy, central cluster of stamens.
Look for the decorative, fluffy, silky seed heads that persist into the autumn and winter.
The presence of 4, 6, or 8 broad 'petals' (which are botanically sepals) is a key identifier.
You can identify it by its leaves, which are typically compound and arranged in opposite pairs on the stem.
The combination of a climbing vine and large, spectacular, simple-but-showy flowers is definitive.
Unlike a climbing rose, a clematis has no thorns.
The central boss of stamens is often a different color from the sepals, creating a beautiful, contrasting eye.
Some varieties have smaller, bell-shaped or urn-shaped, nodding flowers, which is another form to look for.
The beautiful, vertical stripe or 'bar' down the middle of each sepal is characteristic of many popular hybrid varieties.

hibiscus: A hibiscus is a large, showy, trumpet-shaped flower with five broad, often overlapping petals.
Its most defining feature is the long, prominent, central column (the staminal column) that protrudes dramatically from the throat of the flower.
A hibiscus flower has delicate, paper-thin or crepey-textured petals that are often beautifully ruffled or wavy at the edges.
The plant is a tropical or hardy woody shrub with glossy, toothed, ovate, dark green leaves.
At the very tip of the long, central column are the numerous, pollen-dusted anthers and the distinct, often five-parted, fuzzy stigma.
Visually, a hibiscus is the quintessential tropical flower, a bold, flamboyant, and exotic-looking bloom.
The flowers are huge, often the size of a saucer, and come in a vast range of hot, vibrant colors, including fiery red, sunny yellow, bright orange, and shocking pink.
A hibiscus looks like a beautiful, colorful, floral gramophone, with its wide, flaring bell and its long, central 'needle'.
Many varieties are bicolored or have a contrasting, darker-colored 'eye' deep in the throat of the flower.
Its overall appearance is that of a lush, leafy shrub, decorated with enormous, spectacular, but often short-lived, platter-like blossoms.
You can identify a hibiscus by its very long, prominent, central stamen tube that extends far beyond the petals.
Identify it by its large, five-petaled, trumpet-shaped flower.
Look for the woody, shrubby growth habit and the glossy, toothed, dark green leaves.
The combination of a large, simple, five-petaled funnel and the extremely long, central reproductive column is definitive.
The five-lobed, fuzzy stigma at the very tip of the column is a key identifier upon close inspection.
Its hot, tropical color palette is a good clue.
Unlike a rose, a hibiscus has a simple, single layer of five petals and the unique stamen column.
The individual flowers are famously short-lived, often lasting only a single day, which is a characteristic trait.
You can identify it by its often frilly or ruffled petal edges, which give it a soft, celebratory look.
The 'rose of Sharon' (Hibiscus syriacus) is a hardy type, identified by its smaller, often double flowers and its more upright, vase-like shrub form.

columbine: A columbine is a flower with a unique and complex, two-layered structure, featuring an outer ring of five flat, petal-like sepals and an inner ring of five cupped petals.
Its most distinctive feature is that each of the five inner petals is elongated backwards into a long, elegant, hollow spur, which contains nectar.
A columbine has a delicate, almost ethereal appearance, with the flower often nodding or dangling from a slender, wiry stem.
The flowers are often beautifully bicolored, with the outer sepals being a different color from the inner, spurred petals.
The plant has very attractive, lacy, blue-green foliage, with the leaves divided into small, rounded, clover-like lobes.
Visually, a columbine is a plant of intricate, fairy-like beauty, its form a complex assembly of spurs, cups, and sepals.
From the center of the flower, a cluster of numerous, bright yellow stamens protrudes, creating a bright, brushy focal point.
A columbine looks like a small, intricate, five-pointed star with a beautiful, five-parted crown set inside it, complete with five elegant, trailing ribbons (the spurs).
The spurs can be long and straight, or short and curved, depending on the species.
Its overall appearance is that of a graceful, airy, woodland wildflower, with its delicate flowers dancing high above its ferny foliage.
You can identify a columbine by the five distinctive, long, nectar-filled spurs that extend from the back of the flower.
Identify it by its two-layered flower structure: an outer set of five flat sepals and an inner set of five cup-like, spurred petals.
Look for the lacy, blue-green, clover-like foliage that forms a low mound.
The bicolored nature of many varieties, with the inner and outer whorls in contrasting colors, is a key identifier.
The delicate, nodding or dangling posture of the flowers on their thin, airy stems is a good clue.
The prominent, brushy cluster of yellow stamens in the center of the flower is a consistent feature.
The combination of the spurred, bell-like inner petals and the star-like outer sepals is definitive.
Unlike a fuchsia, which also has a two-layered, dangling flower, a columbine has the unique, long nectar spurs.
Its complex, almost geometric form is very recognizable.
After flowering, it forms a cluster of five, pointed, papery seed pods, which is another identifying feature.
A columbine looks like a tiny, magical, five-parted lantern, with elegant, decorative tails trailing behind.

desert-rose: A desert-rose is a succulent plant with a dramatically swollen, bulbous, greyish-tan trunk, called a caudex, that looks like a bonsai tree.
It has simple, trumpet-shaped or funnel-shaped flowers with five broad, pointed or rounded petals.

The most striking feature of a desert-rose (Adenium) is the stark contrast between its thick, gnarled, sculptural, leafless-looking trunk and the delicate, brightly colored flowers that bloom at the tips of its shorter branches.
The flowers are incredibly vibrant, typically in shades of brilliant pink, red, and white, and are often bicolored with a darker picotee edge and a pale or yellow throat.
The leaves are leathery, simple, oblong, and glossy green, and they are usually clustered at the tips of the branches, but the plant may be leafless when it blooms.
Visually, it is a plant of bizarre, sculptural, and exotic beauty, a living piece of natural bonsai art.
A desert-rose looks like a miniature, ancient baobab tree that has miraculously burst into a profusion of huge, lily-like flowers.
The caudex is smooth and pale, resembling a piece of sun-bleached driftwood or a strange, swollen root exposed to the air.
The flowers have a very clean, graphic appearance, with their clear, often sharp-edged, color patterns.
Its overall appearance is that of a stunning, drought-tolerant specimen, a perfect fusion of a weird succulent and a beautiful flowering shrub.
You can identify a desert-rose by its massively swollen, bottle-shaped, pale grey trunk or caudex.
Identify it by the vibrant, trumpet-shaped flowers, often pink or red with a white throat, that bloom from the tips of the thick branches.
Look for the combination of a succulent, bonsai-like base and showy, lily-like flowers.
The way the flowers often appear when the plant has few or no leaves is a key identifier.
The thick, stubby, tapering branches that arise from the main caudex are a characteristic feature.
Its use as a popular, potted, sun-loving specimen plant, especially for bonsai enthusiasts, is a good clue.
The leathery, glossy, simple leaves clustered at the branch tips are another identifier.
Unlike a true rose, the desert-rose has a simple, five-petaled, trumpet shape, a succulent trunk, and no thorns.
The sharp, picotee edge of a darker color on the petals is common in many hybrid varieties.
A milky sap, which is poisonous, will exude if the stem is broken.

tree mallow: A tree mallow is a large, fast-growing, woody shrub that is covered in a profusion of beautiful, hibiscus-like, saucer-shaped flowers.
It has flowers with five large, delicate, paper-thin petals that are often beautifully veined with a darker color.
A tree mallow flower is typically a soft, satiny pink or lavender, with a distinctive, darker, magenta or purple, veined 'eye' at the center.
The five petals are broad and slightly overlapping, often with a notch at the tip, creating a shallow, open-faced bowl.
The plant has soft, velvety, gray-green leaves that are palmate (hand-shaped), with three to seven shallow lobes, resembling a maple leaf.
Visually, it is a plant of incredible, long-lasting, floral abundance, a large, billowy shrub that blooms for months on end.
From the center of the flower, a prominent, brushy, pale-colored stamen column emerges, similar to but shorter than a hibiscus.
A tree mallow looks like a softer, more delicate, and more prolific version of a hibiscus.
The texture of the petals is like fine silk or crêpe paper, and they are often translucent when backlit by the sun.
Its overall appearance is that of a large, soft, rounded, grey-green shrub, constantly decorated with dozens of its beautiful, cup-shaped, purple-veined flowers.
You can identify a tree mallow (Lavatera) by its large, shrubby, semi-woody habit and its profusion of hibiscus-like flowers.
Identify it by the five-petaled, saucer-shaped flowers that have distinctive, dark purple or maroon veining radiating from the center.
Look for the soft, fuzzy, gray-green, maple-leaf-shaped leaves.
The combination of a large, bushy form and a continuous display of pink or lilac, dark-eyed, mallow-type flowers is a key identifier.
Unlike a hibiscus, which often has glossy leaves, a tree mallow's leaves are typically soft, downy, and grayish.
The central stamen column is less prominent than that of a hibiscus.
Its fast, sometimes gangly, growth habit is a characteristic of the plant.
The delicate, paper-thin quality of the petals is a good clue.
You can identify it by its very long blooming season, often from early summer until the first frost.
After the flower fades, it leaves behind a small, button-like, wheel-shaped seed pod, which is typical of the mallow family.

magnolia: A magnolia is a tree or large shrub that produces spectacular, large, cup-shaped or star-shaped flowers, often before the leaves emerge in spring.
It has flowers with numerous large, thick, waxy, petal-like structures called tepals, not distinct petals and sepals.
A magnolia flower has a primitive, elegant, and sculptural form, with its broad, smooth tepals forming a beautiful, chalice-like bloom.
The center of the flower is a prominent, cone-shaped or pineapple-shaped structure, which is the aggregate of the stamens and pistils.
The flowers are often a pristine, pure white or come in beautiful shades of pale to deep pink, creamy yellow, and rich, claret-purple.
Visually, a magnolia in bloom is a breathtaking spectacle, its bare, sculptural branches decorated with hundreds of huge, luminous, goblet-shaped flowers.
The tepals are substantial and almost fleshy or leathery to the touch, which helps them hold their elegant, cup-like shape.
A magnolia looks like a tree full of beautiful, giant, porcelain teacups or magnificent, luminous water lilies.
After the flower fades, the central cone develops into a bizarre, cone-like fruit, which may split open to reveal bright red or orange seeds.
Its overall appearance is one of ancient, timeless, and sophisticated beauty, a true aristocrat of the plant world.
You can identify a magnolia by its large, cup-shaped or star-shaped flowers with many thick, waxy tepals that bloom in early spring.
Identify it by its habit of flowering on bare, woody branches before the leaves appear.
Look for the prominent, cone-like structure of stamens and pistils in the center of the flower.
The large, fuzzy, gray or brown, pussy-willow-like flower buds that form in winter are a key identifier for the spring-blooming types.
The smooth, gray bark of the tree is another characteristic feature.
The combination of a woody tree or shrub and huge, simple, multi-tepaled flowers is definitive.
Unlike a cherry or apple blossom, which has small, thin petals, a magnolia's flower parts are very large, thick, and substantial.
The unique, cone-like fruit that develops after flowering is a good clue.
The large, simple, often leathery leaves (which appear after the flowers) are another identifier.
The saucer-shaped, star-shaped, or goblet-shaped form of the large flower is its most recognizable trait.
A magnolia in bloom looks like a beautiful, candelabra tree, each branch tip holding a large, glowing, floral flame.

cyclamen: A cyclamen is a delicate plant with distinctive, swept-back (reflexed) petals that make the flower look like a butterfly or a shooting star.
A cyclamen features nodding, elegant flowers that rise on slender stems from a clump of heart-shaped leaves.
It has beautiful, heart-shaped or kidney-shaped leaves that are often intricately patterned with silvery-green or pewter markings.
A cyclamen flower has five upswept petals, creating a silhouette that resembles a shuttlecock, and it typically blooms in shades of pink, red, purple, or white.
The flower has a downward-pointing 'snout' and petals that flare sharply upwards and backwards, often with a darker magenta blotch at the base.
A cyclamen's visual appeal comes from its graceful, nodding flowers and its highly decorative, silver-patterned, heart-shaped foliage.
It is a small, neat plant with flowers held in a cluster above a low-growing rosette of patterned leaves.
A cyclamen looks like a collection of delicate, flying butterflies, their wings swept back, hovering over a bed of patterned hearts.
The petals are smooth and often twisted slightly, giving the flower a sense of elegant motion.
It is a tuberous plant where the flowers, with their fully reflexed petals, rise on thin, leafless stalks.
You can identify a cyclamen by its unique flowers with five petals that are sharply swept backwards, away from the flower's mouth.
Identify it by its heart-shaped leaves, which nearly always have beautiful, silvery or light green patterns on a dark green background.
Look for the nodding or drooping posture of the flowers on their slender stems.
The combination of the reflexed petals and the patterned, heart-shaped leaves is the most definitive way to identify a cyclamen.
You can identify it by its growth from a round, flattened corm, which often sits near the surface of the soil.
Unlike most flowers, the petals of a cyclamen point upwards and backwards, not outwards.
The dark, purplish 'mouth' at the base of the petals is a characteristic of many florist's cyclamen varieties.
Its typical appearance as a compact, winter-blooming potted plant is a common way to recognize it.

watercress: A watercress is an aquatic or semi-aquatic plant with creeping or floating stems and leaves made up of small, rounded, dark green leaflets.
A watercress looks like a lush, dense, green mat of foliage, often found floating on the surface of clear, slow-moving streams or ponds.
It has compound leaves, with several pairs of small, oval or nearly circular leaflets arranged along a central stalk, with a single, larger leaflet at the tip.
A watercress produces small, four-petaled, pure white flowers that are held in small, upright clusters (racemes) just above the water or the leaves.
The stems are typically hollow and light green, and they root easily at the nodes where they touch mud or water.
Visually, it is a plant of vibrant, edible green, its primary feature being its mass of succulent-looking, rounded leaves.
The leaflets are smooth-edged and have a deep, rich green color, indicating its nutrient-rich nature.
A watercress plant often forms dense, tangled beds in shallow water, its leaves providing a verdant surface.
It looks like a miniature, aquatic version of a nasturtium, with its rounded leaflets and creeping habit.
Its overall appearance is that of a fresh, clean, and lush green vegetable, thriving in pristine water.
You can identify a watercress by its aquatic habitat, growing directly in or at the edge of clear, running water.
Identify it by its compound leaves, which are composed of several small, distinctly rounded or oval leaflets.
Look for the small clusters of simple, four-petaled, white flowers, which have a cross-like shape.
Breaking a stem will reveal that it is hollow, which is a key identifier.
You can identify watercress by its distinctive, peppery smell when a leaf is crushed.
The combination of a creeping, aquatic habit and leaves made of multiple round leaflets is definitive.
Unlike many other aquatic plants, watercress has these characteristic, edible-looking, pinnate leaves, not long grasses or broad lily pads.
The small, delicate, white flowers rising just above the green mat of leaves are a good clue.
Its tendency to form dense, sprawling colonies in its preferred habitat helps in identification.

canna lily: A canna lily is a tall, tropical-looking plant with very large, broad, paddle-shaped leaves that resemble those of a banana tree.
It has a stout, upright, unbranched stalk that bears a terminal spike of large, brightly colored, asymmetrical flowers.
A canna lily's flowers are composed of showy, petal-like structures (staminodes), and they are often ruffled and flamboyant, not simple and symmetrical.
The plant's most dramatic feature is its lush, bold, tropical foliage, which can be pure green, bronze, deep burgundy, or striped with various colors.
The flowers come in a fiery palette of hot, vibrant colors, including scarlet red, brilliant orange, sunny yellow, and warm pink.
Visually, it is a plant of bold, architectural, and unapologetically exotic beauty, a true centerpiece of the summer garden.
A canna lily looks like a more robust and dramatic version of a gladiolus, but with huge, tropical leaves.
The flowers are irregular in shape, with several broad, unequal, often frilly 'petals' creating a complex, orchid-like bloom.
Its overall appearance is that of a majestic, jungle-like plant, bringing a powerful vertical element and a lush, tropical feel to the landscape.
The large leaves unfurl from the central stalk in a beautiful, spiraling fashion.
You can identify a canna lily by its enormous, banana-like or paddle-shaped leaves.
Identify it by the tall, thick stalk that is topped with a spike of large, brightly colored, irregular-shaped flowers.
Look for the lush, tropical appearance, especially the foliage which can be green, bronze, or variegated.
Unlike a true lily, a canna's flowers are asymmetrical, with large, modified stamens (staminodes) forming the showy 'petals'.
The combination of the huge, paddle-like leaves and the terminal cluster of fiery, orchid-like flowers is definitive.
Its growth from a thick, fleshy rhizome is another identifier.
The large, often colorful, leaf sheaths that wrap around the main stem are a characteristic feature.
You can identify it by its bold, hot-colored flowers in shades of red, orange, and yellow.

hippeastrum: A hippeastrum is a plant with enormous, spectacular, trumpet-shaped flowers that are borne in a cluster at the top of a tall, thick, hollow stalk.
It has very large, six-petaled flowers (three petals and three sepals that look alike), which can be up to 10 inches across.
A hippeastrum's most famous feature is its massive bulb, from which a single, leafless, statuesque flower scape emerges, typically bearing two to six huge blooms.
The flowers come in a rich array of colors, including deep, velvety reds, pure whites, soft pinks, and salmon-oranges, and are often beautifully striped, veined, or edged in contrasting colors.
The plant has long, strap-like, deep green, fleshy leaves that usually appear after or during flowering.
Visually, a hippeastrum (commonly sold as amaryllis) is a plant of jaw-dropping, theatrical beauty, a true diva of the indoor winter garden.
The six petals are broad and often have a beautiful, crystalline sheen, and they curve gracefully backwards to form a wide, open-faced trumpet.
A hippeastrum looks like a magnificent, oversized lily, but it grows from a giant bulb on a single, thick, hollow stem.
Inside the flower's throat, there are long, elegant, curving stamens and a prominent, three-lobed stigma.
Its overall appearance is that of a simple, yet incredibly dramatic, composition: a tall, straight stalk, a cluster of huge, perfect trumpets, and a base of arching, strap-like leaves.
You can identify a hippeastrum by its cluster of huge, trumpet-shaped flowers at the top of a single, thick, leafless, hollow scape.
Identify it by the very large, onion-like bulb from which the flower stalk and leaves emerge.
Look for the long, strap-shaped, arching, green leaves that are typical of the plant.
The enormous size of the individual flowers is a key identifier.
The combination of a leafless, hollow stalk and a terminal umbel of two to six giant, six-petaled flowers is definitive.
Unlike a true lily, which grows outdoors from a scaly bulb and has leaves along its stem, a hippeastrum grows from a tunicate bulb and has a leafless scape.
The way the flowers face outwards, often in a 360-degree arrangement, is a recognizable trait.
The beautiful patterns, such as the white, star-like blaze in the throat of a red flower, or the fine, picotee edge on the petals, are good clues for specific varieties.
Its common use as a spectacular, forced, indoor-blooming plant during the winter holidays is how many people recognize it.
The six long, prominent stamens and the single, long style are visible in the center of the open trumpet.

bee balm: A bee balm is a plant with a unique and unconventional flower head that is a shaggy, crown-like, or spiky ball of thin, tubular flowers.
It has a distinctive, two-tiered flower structure, with a ring of colorful, leaf-like bracts at the base, from which a dense, globular head of two-lipped, tubular florets emerges.
A bee balm flower looks like a miniature, colorful firework, a beautiful, ragged explosion of slender tubes.
The plant is a member of the mint family, and as such, it has characteristic square (four-sided) stems and aromatic, toothed, lance-shaped leaves arranged in opposite pairs.
The flower heads are held at the top of the tall, sturdy stems, and come in vibrant shades of red, pink, magenta, and purple.
Visually, a bee balm (or Monarda) is a plant of wild, informal, and striking beauty, a magnet for pollinators.
The individual flowers are long, thin tubes that split into an upper and lower 'lip', giving the whole head a very shaggy, unkempt, yet beautiful appearance.
A bee balm looks like a beautiful, punk-rock hairdo, a spiky, colorful mohawk of a flower.
The cluster of bracts below the flower head is often a similar, if paler, color to the flowers, adding to the overall colorful effect.
Its overall appearance is that of a tall, upright, clumping perennial, with its fascinating, spiky, globe-like flower heads providing a unique texture in the garden.
You can identify a bee balm by its unique, shaggy, globe-shaped flower head, which is composed of many slender, tubular flowers.
Identify it by its square stems, a clear indicator that it is in the mint family.
Look for the aromatic leaves, which have a pleasant, minty or oregano-like scent when crushed.
The firework-like or crown-like appearance of the flower head is its most definitive visual feature.
The combination of a shaggy, terminal flower head, square stems, and opposite leaves is the key identifier.
Unlike a salvia, which has flowers arranged in a long, tapered spike, a bee balm's flowers are in a dense, rounded, terminal head.
Its attraction to bees, hummingbirds, and butterflies is a well-known characteristic that gives it its name.
You can identify it by the ring of leafy bracts that sits directly underneath the flower ball.
The two-lipped shape of the individual, slender flowers is a good clue upon close inspection.
After the petals fall, the remaining round, brown, button-like seed head is also a recognizable feature.

ball moss: A ball moss is not a moss, but an epiphytic plant (a type of tillandsia) that forms a small, dense, ball-shaped or hemispherical clump of silvery-gray, thread-like leaves.
It has a very tangled, wiry, and almost chaotic appearance, looking like a small, gray-green tumbleweed or a bird's nest.
A ball moss is rootless, and it clings to the branches of trees, telephone wires, or fences using small, wiry holdfasts called trichomes, but it is not a parasite.
The leaves are very slender, stiff, and curled, and they are covered in tiny, silvery-gray scales that help the plant absorb water and nutrients from the air.
It produces a single, small, inconspicuous flower on a short stalk from the center of the clump, which is usually a pale violet or blue.
Visually, a ball moss is a strange and fascinating organism, a small, independent, aerial puffball of life.
It looks like a small, spiky, gray-green sea urchin that has decided to live in a tree.
The overall color is a dusty, silvery-green or gray, which allows it to blend in with the bark of many trees.
A ball moss appears as a small, dense, seemingly leafless clump from a distance, but up close, its structure of individual, tangled leaves is visible.
Its form is a tight, spherical cluster, typically ranging from the size of a golf ball to that of a softball.
You can identify a ball moss by its dense, ball-shaped clump of silvery-gray, wiry leaves.
Identify it by its epiphytic habitat, growing on the branches of other trees (especially live oaks) and on man-made structures, without being rooted in them.
It is not a moss; it is a flowering plant, which can be identified if the small, violet flower is present, but the vegetative clump is the main feature.
The gray, scaly, almost dusty-looking surface of the leaves is a key identifier.
Unlike Spanish moss, which hangs in long, draping festoons, ball moss grows in distinct, tight, ball-like clumps.
The tangled, almost messy, bird's nest-like structure of the clump is a good clue.
You can identify it by its rootless nature; the entire organism can be easily detached from its host.
The stiff, curled, thread-like leaves are a primary visual trait.
Its presence in humid, subtropical climates, such as the southern United States and Latin America, helps in its identification.
The small size and spherical shape of the individual clumps are consistent and recognizable.
A ball moss looks like a small, gray, living pom-pom or a tiny, natural, aerial hedgehog.
What a ball moss looks like is a small, dusty starburst, a frozen firework of wiry, gray-green threads.
It has a very tough, resilient, and almost prehistoric appearance, a simple organism that has mastered a difficult way of life.
A ball moss looks like a handful of tangled, gray, fishing line that has been wadded up and thrown into a tree.

foxglove: A foxglove is a tall, majestic plant with a towering, one-sided spire of large, downward-hanging, bell-shaped or thimble-shaped flowers.
Its most notable feature is the beautiful, speckled or freckled pattern of dark purple or maroon spots inside the throat of each bell-like flower.
A foxglove has a stately, architectural presence, with its single, tall, dramatic flower spike rising from a large, basal rosette of soft, hairy leaves.
The individual flowers are large, tubular, and have a wide mouth, resembling the finger of a glove, hence the name.
The plant forms a low-growing clump of large, soft, grayish-green, ovate leaves with a downy or woolly texture in its first year, before sending up the flower spike.
Visually, a foxglove is the quintessential cottage garden flower, a perfect blend of stately grace and wild, natural beauty.
The flowers are arranged in a dense raceme, and they bloom sequentially from the bottom of the spike to the top, with the tip being a cluster of small, unopened buds.
A foxglove looks like a beautiful, floral chandelier, with dozens of spotted, bell-like lampshades hanging down.
The colors are typically in a regal palette of purple, lavender, rosy pink, creamy white, and apricot yellow.
Its overall appearance is strongly vertical, a magnificent, colorful tower that provides a powerful accent in the garden.
You can identify a foxglove by its tall, upright spike of large, drooping, tubular or bell-shaped flowers.
Identify it by the distinctive, dark speckles or spots inside the throat of the flowers.
Look for the large, soft, hairy, grayish-green leaves that form a rosette at the base of the plant.
The combination of the tall, one-sided flower spike and the large, basal rosette of leaves is a key identifier.
The thimble-like or 'finger-of-a-glove' shape of the individual flowers is a definitive trait.
Unlike a delphinium or lupine, which have spires of small, open-faced flowers, a foxglove has large, drooping, bell-shaped blooms.
Its biennial life cycle (a leafy rosette the first year, a flower spike the second) is a characteristic feature.
The way the flowers all hang on one side of the stem is a good clue.
The soft, fuzzy texture of the leaves and stems is another identifying trait.

bougainvillea: A bougainvillea is a rampant, woody, climbing vine that is covered in an overwhelming profusion of intensely colored, papery, leaf-like structures, which are its bracts.
Its most recognized feature is not its flower, but these three large, triangular, paper-thin bracts that surround the true flower.

The true flowers of a bougainvillea are very small, inconspicuous, creamy-white or yellow, star-shaped, and tubular, and they are always found in a cluster of three in the center of the three colorful bracts.
The plant has woody, thorny stems that allow it to scramble and climb over walls, fences, and other structures.
The colorful bracts come in an incredible range of hot, vibrant colors, including shocking magenta, deep purple, fiery red, brilliant orange, and pure white.
Visually, a bougainvillea is a spectacular, sun-drenched, tropical cascade, a living waterfall of pure, intense, papery color.
The bracts have a delicate, crinkled, tissue-paper-like texture and prominent veins.
A bougainvillea looks like a magnificent, colorful, paper explosion, a plant that has decided to become a permanent festival.
The leaves are simple, heart-shaped or ovate, and bright green, and they are often completely obscured by the masses of colored bracts during peak bloom.
Its overall appearance is that of a tough, drought-tolerant, sun-loving vine that creates a breathtaking, solid sheet of color.
You can identify a bougainvillea by its masses of colorful, paper-thin bracts, which are often mistaken for flowers.
Identify it by looking for the tiny, white or yellow, tubular true flowers that are nestled in the center of the three colored bracts.
Look for the woody, often very thorny, scrambling and climbing stems.
The combination of a rampant, thorny vine and its profusion of papery, brightly colored bracts is definitive.
Its intense, tropical color palette—especially the iconic, vibrant magenta—is a key identifier.
Unlike a climbing rose, which has complex flowers, a bougainvillea's color comes from the simple, leaf-like bracts.
The crinkled, tissue-paper texture of the bracts is a good clue.
Its preference for hot, sunny, and dry climates is characteristic, and it is a common sight in Mediterranean and tropical regions.
The structure of 'three bracts surrounding three tiny flowers' is a consistent and identifiable feature.

camellia: A camellia is a flower with a perfect, geometric structure, featuring layers of waxy, overlapping petals that form a pristine rosette.
A camellia has lush, glossy, dark evergreen leaves that provide a stark, beautiful contrast to its large, soft-colored flowers.
It features a dense, rose-like or peony-like bloom, with petals that are thick, smooth, and feel like cool wax to the touch.
A camellia can have a central boss of prominent, bright, golden-yellow stamens, especially in the single or semi-double varieties.
The flower is a study in perfection, with its flawless, symmetrical arrangement of petals, typically in shades of pure white, soft pink, or deep red.
A camellia is a woody, evergreen shrub or small tree with a dense, handsome structure even when not in bloom.
The petals are broad and rounded, and in 'formal double' types, they are layered in a perfect, mesmerizing spiral with no visible stamens.
A camellia looks like a sculptural masterpiece, its petals so perfectly formed and arranged they seem to be carved from wax or porcelain.
The flower buds are large, round, and scaly, promising the magnificent bloom to come.
Its overall appearance is one of timeless, formal elegance, a classic beauty of the winter and early spring garden.
You can identify a camellia by its thick, waxy, perfectly formed petals, which are very different from the soft, thin petals of a rose.
Identify it by its very glossy, leathery, dark green, evergreen leaves with finely serrated edges.
Look for its bloom time in the cooler months, from late autumn through early spring, when few other shrubs are flowering.
The absence of thorns on its woody stems is a key feature that distinguishes it from a rose.
You can identify a 'formal double' camellia by its perfect, geometric, spiral pattern of petals with no stamens visible.
You can identify a 'single' camellia by its simple, open-faced, five-to-eight-petaled flower with a large, central cluster of bright yellow stamens.
The combination of a large, waxy, multi-petaled flower and glossy, evergreen, serrated leaves is definitive.
After the flower fades, it typically drops from the plant in one whole, intact piece.

mallow: A mallow is a simple, charming flower with a cup-shaped or saucer-shaped bloom, composed of five distinct, often notched or heart-shaped petals.
It has delicate, paper-thin petals that are usually beautifully marked with fine, dark, radiating veins.
A mallow flower features a prominent, central, brush-like column of fused stamens, which is a hallmark of the mallow family.
The plant typically has rounded or palmate (hand-shaped), often slightly fuzzy or downy, green leaves.
The flowers are typically in shades of soft pink, lavender, magenta, or pure white, and they have a delicate, silky sheen.
Visually, a mallow is a plant of simple, rustic, and unfussy beauty, the classic wildflower of the hedgerow or cottage garden.
The five petals are separate and often have a noticeable gap between them, creating a pinwheel-like effect.
A mallow looks like a more delicate and veined version of a hibiscus, with a less dramatic stamen column.
After flowering, it produces a unique, button-like, wheel-shaped seed pod, sometimes called a 'cheese', which is also a distinctive feature.
Its overall appearance is often that of an upright, branching plant, with the pretty, cup-shaped flowers blooming along the stems in the leaf axils.
You can identify a mallow by its simple, five-petaled, cup-shaped flowers with distinctive, darker, radiating veins.
Identify it by the prominent, central column of stamens, which looks like a small, fuzzy brush.
Look for the round, button-like seed pod that resembles a small wheel of cheese, a key feature of the Malva genus.
The palmate or rounded, often slightly hairy leaves are a good identifier for the plant.
The combination of a simple, five-petaled, veined flower and a central stamen column is definitive.
Unlike a geranium, which also has five petals, a mallow has the central stamen column and notched, not rounded, petals.
Its often tall, upright, and somewhat weedy growth habit is characteristic of many common mallows.
The delicate, almost translucent, crepe-paper texture of the petals is a recognizable feature.
You can identify it by looking for a flower with five separate, heart-shaped petals.

mexican petunia: A mexican petunia is an upright, bushy plant that produces a profusion of simple, trumpet-shaped, violet-blue or purple flowers.
It has five delicate, petal-like lobes that flare out from a slender tube, resembling a smaller, simpler version of a garden petunia.
A mexican petunia features long, narrow, lance-shaped, dark green leaves that often have a purplish tint, especially on the stems.
The flowers are borne in clusters in the leaf axils along the upright stems, creating a continuous display of color.
The plant has a very vigorous, almost weedy appearance, forming dense, spreading clumps of dark, leafy stems.
Visually, a mexican petunia is a plant of incredible, non-stop, floral production, a tough and resilient source of cool, purple color.
The flowers are delicate and tissue-paper thin, and each one famously lasts for only a single day, to be replaced by a new one the next morning.
A mexican petunia looks like a small, wild, purple morning glory that has decided to grow as a bush instead of a vine.
The stems are strong, often purplish, and have slightly swollen nodes, giving the plant an architectural, bamboo-like look.
Its overall appearance is that of a dense, dark green and purple hedge, constantly studded with bright, jewel-like, purple trumpet flowers.
You can identify a mexican petunia by its prolific, trumpet-shaped, purple flowers on an upright, bushy plant.
Identify it by its long, narrow, lance-shaped leaves, which are often a dark, glossy green with purple undertones.
Look for the fact that the individual flowers are very short-lived, lasting only a day, so you will often see both fresh flowers and fallen, wilted ones around the plant.
The combination of the purple trumpet flowers, dark lance-shaped leaves, and its aggressive, spreading, clumping habit is a key identifier.
Unlike a true petunia, which is a soft, mounding annual, the Mexican petunia (Ruellia) is a tough, upright, often invasive perennial with darker, narrower leaves.
Its ability to thrive in hot, wet conditions is a clue to its identity.
The simple, five-lobed, open-faced funnel shape of the flower is a primary visual trait.
Look for the long, thin, bean-like seed pods that form after the flowers and often 'explode' to disperse their seeds.
The slightly woody base and the strong, upright, often purplish stems are good identifiers.
Its continuous, tireless blooming from summer through fall is one of its most recognizable characteristics.
A mexican petunia looks like a beautiful, but relentless, river of purple, a plant that is determined to bloom.

bromelia: A bromelia is an exotic, tropical plant that forms a rosette of stiff, leathery, often strap-like leaves.
Its most dramatic feature is the spectacular, brightly colored flower spike (inflorescence) that rises from the center of the leafy rosette.
A bromelia's 'flower' is actually composed of showy, colorful, long-lasting bracts, while the true flowers are small and often hidden within these bracts.
The central leaves of the rosette form a 'cup' or 'tank' that is designed to hold water.
The leaves can be solid green, or beautifully variegated with stripes, bands, or spots of cream, pink, or red, and they often have spiny edges.
Visually, a bromelia is a living piece of architectural, tropical sculpture, with a form that is both alien and beautiful.
The flower spike can take many forms: a fiery, red, star-shaped burst (Guzmania), a flattened, pink, feather-like paddle (Vriesea), or a bizarre, spiky club (Aechmea).
A bromelia looks like a fantastical, prehistoric plant, a perfect fusion of a succulent and a bizarre, colorful flower.
Many bromeliads are epiphytes, and their sparse, wiry root systems are primarily for anchoring the plant to a tree, not for absorbing nutrients.
Its overall appearance is that of a tough, self-sufficient, and incredibly striking inhabitant of the jungle.
You can identify a bromelia by its rosette of stiff, strap-like leaves that form a central water-holding cup.
Identify it by the dramatic, colorful, central inflorescence, which is made of long-lasting bracts, not soft petals.
Look for its epiphytic habit, often seen mounted on pieces of wood or growing in trees in its native environment.
The spiny or serrated edges of the stiff, leathery leaves are a key identifier for many species.
The combination of a leafy, vase-like plant and a central, bizarre, colorful flower spike is definitive.
Unlike a typical flowering plant, a bromeliad's main attraction is often its foliage and bracts, with the true flowers being small and short-lived.
The 'pup' or offset that grows from the base of the mother plant after it flowers is a characteristic of its life cycle.
You can identify it by looking for the tiny, true flowers, often blue or yellow, peeking out from between the showy, colorful bracts.
The often variegated or banded patterns on the leaves themselves are a good clue.
Its unique, almost alien, and highly sculptural form is its most recognizable trait.
A bromelia looks like a beautiful, living, tropical vase, its leaves forming the vessel and the flower spike being the magnificent bouquet.
What a bromelia looks like is an explosion of tropical color, a starburst of fiery red or hot pink bracts frozen in time.
It has a very modern, dramatic, and architectural appearance, a plant that serves as a living work of art.
A bromelia looks like a creature from another planet, a beautiful, symbiotic organism of leaf and flower.

blanket flower: A blanket flower is a vibrant, daisy-like wildflower with petals that are typically a fiery, bicolored combination of red and yellow.
It has a distinctive ray of petals that are red or brownish-red at the base and tipped with a brilliant, sunny yellow.

The ray petals have a unique shape; they are often tubular at the base and flare out into a flat, three-toothed tip.
The plant is a tough, drought-tolerant perennial with a slightly sprawling habit and rough, hairy, grayish-green, lance-shaped leaves.

The flower heads are held singly on long, slender stems, which allows them to nod and dance in the wind.

trumpet creeper: A trumpet creeper is a vigorous, woody, climbing vine that produces large clusters of spectacular, trumpet-shaped flowers.
It has large, 2- to 4-inch-long, funnel-shaped flowers that are typically a brilliant, fiery, reddish-orange with a yellowish throat.
A trumpet creeper is a powerful vine that clings to surfaces using small, aerial rootlets that grow from its woody stems.
The plant has attractive, pinnately compound leaves, each composed of numerous, toothed, pointed, dark green leaflets.
The flowers are borne in terminal clusters of 4 to 12, creating a large, showy mass of hot color at the ends of the branches.
Visually, it is a plant of rampant, almost aggressive, tropical-looking beauty, a true spectacle of the summer.
The flower is a long tube that flares out into five shallow, rounded lobes, creating a wide, open mouth.
A trumpet creeper looks like a beautiful, but overwhelming, cascade of fiery, orange horns.
After flowering, it produces long, bean-like or cigar-shaped, green-to-brown seed pods that can be up to 6 inches long.
Its overall appearance is that of a fast-growing, dense, leafy vine that, in summer, is set ablaze with its brilliant, orange-red blooms.
You can identify a trumpet creeper by its large, trumpet-shaped, reddish-orange flowers that grow in clusters.
Identify it by its aggressive, climbing habit, using aerial rootlets to cling tightly to walls, fences, and trees.
Look for the pinnately compound leaves, which are made up of 7 to 11 toothed leaflets.
The long, bean-like or cigar-shaped seed pods that hang from the vine in late summer and fall are a key identifier.
The combination of a woody, climbing vine, compound leaves, and large, orange, trumpet-shaped flowers is definitive.
Its powerful attraction for hummingbirds is a well-known characteristic.
Unlike a honeysuckle, which also has a trumpet-like flower, the trumpet creeper's blooms are much larger and are a fiery orange-red.
The thick, woody, rope-like stems of a mature vine are a good clue.
Its ability to cover large structures like telephone poles or entire walls is a testament to its vigorous nature.

blackberry lily: A blackberry lily is an iris-like plant with a fan of flat, sword-shaped leaves and a small, star-shaped, orange flower that is heavily speckled with dark red or purple spots.
Its most unique and defining feature is the seed pod, which splits open in the fall to reveal a dense, spherical cluster of shiny, black seeds that looks exactly like a ripe blackberry.
The flower is small and flat-faced, with six equal-sized, pointed petals that create a perfect, six-pointed star.
The plant has a slender, branching flower stalk that rises from the fan of leaves and carries numerous, short-lived, spotted flowers.
The base color of the flower is a bright, clear orange, and it is covered in a dense, leopard-like pattern of dark red spots.
Visually, it is a plant of two distinct and equally fascinating seasons of beauty: the delicate, spotted flowers of summer, and the bizarre, berry-like seed clusters of autumn.
A blackberry lily looks like a miniature, orange, leopard-spotted daylily.
The leaves are arranged in a flat, vertical plane, creating a fan-like structure that is very typical of the iris family.
Each flower lasts for only a day, but the plant produces them in succession over a long period.
Its overall appearance is that of a graceful, airy, and unusual perennial, prized for both its flowers and its even more remarkable seeds.
You can identify a blackberry lily most easily by its seed cluster, which is a perfect, shiny, black replica of a blackberry.
Identify it by its small, flat, six-petaled, orange flowers that are heavily spotted with red.
Look for the iris-like fan of flat, sword-shaped leaves.
The combination of the iris-like foliage, the small, spotted, star-shaped flowers, and the unique seed cluster is definitive.
Unlike a true lily or a daylily, the blackberry lily's flowers are smaller, flatter, and always spotted.
The way the green seed pod peels back its three sections to reveal the black 'berry' inside is a key identifying process.
Its slender, wiry, branching flower stems give the plant an airy quality, which is a good clue.
Its bloom time in mid- to late-summer is characteristic.
The perfect, six-pointed, star-like symmetry of the flat, open flower is a primary visual trait.
The contrast between the hot, fiery colors of the flower and the cool, almost reptilian look of the seed cluster is a memorable feature.
